items.title: CFP: 3rd International Interdisciplinary Conference of the Department of English, University of Zululand 28-30 September 2026 Re-imagining Language, W
items.location: University of Zululand, Ongoye, KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa · University of Zululand
items.excerpt: CFP: 3rd International Interdisciplinary Conference of the Department of English, University of Zululand 28-30 September 2026 Re-imagining Language, Writing, and Literature in English in a Fast-Changing World In an era that has been labelled by scholars as the age of technocracy, AI and post-truth, the spheres of literature and the channels of creative an...
items.fullDescription: CFP: 3rd International Interdisciplinary Conference of the Department of English, University of Zululand 28-30 September 2026 Re-imagining Language, Writing, and Literature in English in a Fast-Changing World In an era that has been labelled by scholars as the age of technocracy, AI and post-truth, the spheres of literature and the channels of creative and critical discourses seem to face a dire future. Society globally is becoming entrapped within an accelerated spiral comprising lowest common denominator discourse, the loss of autonomy, the dissipation of embodied social networks and the forceful commodification of imagination and creativity. Considering these threats, it is vital to create radical acts of imagining and reimagining. These should be rooted not only in the hegemonic technocratic traditions and orthodoxies imposed from the Global North, but also, and more urgently, in the lived textures of local language, memory, and survival. The need to re-imagine language, writing and literature is not only an aesthetic necessity, but a desperate need for the future of meaning and society. In the context of AI for example, there are thriving, often cacophonous discourses debating the extent to which the technological deployment of large language models (LLMs) either enhance or curtail individual expression whether in private contexts of self-articulation or in the more public spheres of formal education. In the sphere of formal education, the issue of ethics has assumed a significant role as reliance on, or use of largely predictive AI-enabled or AI-generated content challenges previously stable notions of academic integrity and authentic authorship. While there seems to be consensus on the need for the 'ethical' use of AI in these contexts, the question that continues to divide commentators is: What exactly constitutes 'ethical' use of content or material that is generated or curated in full or part by another party? What exactly is ethical these days? Other vital questions relate to the dominance of certain languages (European) and what seems to be the increased sidelining of others (African in particular) as well as the unsubtly hidden market-centric orientation of the current drive towards AI in academic writing. These are only a few of the significant puzzles posed by the new forms of language-use inaugurated, imposed and valorised by new forms of technology. This invites scrutiny of the more traditional roles that language plays in embodying culture, collective identity, knowledge-creation and transmission as well as in the codification of epistemologies. Given the marginalisation of indigenous languages and knowledge systems in contemporary Africa over the centuries of colonial domination, reclaiming language as a medium to address unabating local dilemmas is proving to be more daunting than ever. Yet, the rise of social media as de-facto sites of imagination and new forms of expression represent possibilities for cultural expression and practices of literature and knowledge production. Furthermore, the increasing diversity of social platforms spawned by technology has a variety of positive and negative implications for, among other things, the re-imagination of fiction, redefining realism, generating alternate currents of speculation, enforcing modes of stereotyping, unforeseen ontological hallucinations, and even contributing towards the current vivification of propaganda. All in all, there is no simple reductionist approach to interpret the current moment. Considering the urgency of the above, the Department of English at the University of Zululand issues the following call for papers: Re-imagining Language, Writing, and Literature in English in a Fast-Changing World Themes and sub-themes which are encouraged (but not limited to) include: The future of language and literature. Imagination and conflicts/interfaces with reality. Academic ethics, authorship and linguistic agency in the age of AI. Ghost Writing and Co-creation Home, homelessness, journeys and writing. Dreaming and language. Multilingualism and Integration Imaginations in literature as forms of resistance and rebellion. Postcolonial literatures, planetarity and the global technocratic order. Imagination and the narrating of already lived (possibly forgotten) histories. Fading/submerged names of nations, peoples, and things, and the (non)realms of departed/buried ontologies. Cracks in reality and meaning. Re-telling and translations Lost childhoods and lost generational mandates/missions. Non-existence as an outcome of personal and social life. Survival and language. Ecological imaginations and environmental futures. The death of nature/ecological unravellings in literary texts Extinctions and disappearances of forms of literature and narratives. Otherness, adaptation and change Possible subversive/creative uses of AI and technology as imagination tools. Slavery, Exploitation, and AI. Slow writing and slow reading as forms of accelerationist resistance and critique. Furthermore, we open the call to other interpretations of the theme which weave academic insights with creative interpretations, including: Short presentations of lived practice of language imaginings, writers' reflections upon their own creative practice(s) which may blur lines between creative and academic spaces. Approaches to creative writing which may enhance imaginative possibilities. Presentations of short pieces of creative writing which directly respond to the theme/sub-themes. Performances of language reimagining or embodied writing. Provocations/perturbations of the possibilities/impossibilities of language and meaning when situated within structured genres/spaces/histories. Literary manifestoes of a "decline to participate" (in the mode of Lesego Rampolokeng, in 2014) in the dominant futurity narrative (challenging the hegemonic rhetoric, adapt to 4IR or be irrelevant) with an insistence on alternative unlived narratives and subversive explorations of change. We invite all interested parties to submit abstracts of approximately 250 words, with five keywords, by 31 July 2026, to the following three email addresses: allank@unizulu.ac.za / phakathib@unizulu.ac.za / ndlovua@unizulu.ac.za If accepted, a member of the organising team will contact you prior to 31 July 2026. The Organising Team, 3rd International Interdisciplinary Conference of the Department of English, University of Zululand.

This scientific event will be held in Rabat-Morocco, on 28-29 October2026, under the theme: "Human-Centered STEM Ecosystems and AI-Driven Technological Disruptions:Rethinking Engineering, Higher Education and Schooling in an Era of Uncertainty". Scope and Objectives Artificial intelligence and rapid technological innovation are profoundly reshaping educationsystems worldwide. In an era of uncertainty and complexity, STEM education must go beyondtechnical skills to embrace humanistic values, ethics, sustainability and social responsibility.The CIMQUSEF/EVALSUP 2026 Conference provides an international forum for researchers,educators, practitioners and policymakers to explore emerging paradigms in STEMecosystems, AI-driven innovation and engineering education. Submission Guidelines All papers must be original and not simultaneously submitted to another journal or conference. Proposals for papers may be oral presentations, round-tables or workshops. The abstract should describe the problem, objectives, methodology and main results of the proposal. Topic 2 - STEM Ecosystems and Educational Policies : Governance, partnerships, institutional strategies, internationalization. Topic 3 - Innovative Pedagogies and Didactics in STEM : Interdisciplinary approaches, active learning, teacher education. Topic 4 - Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technologies in Education : Generative AI, learning analytics, VR/AR, digital learning resources. Topic 5 - Engineering Education in an Era of Uncertainty and AI Disruptions : Future engineering competencies, systems thinking, ethics, curriculum transformation. Topic 6 - Equity, Inclusion and Diversity in STEM : Gender equity, inclusive practices, access and student success. Topic 7 - Research, Innovation and Entrepreneurship in Education : Knowledge transfer, innovation culture, maker-spaces, entrepreneurship. Topic 8 - Quality Assurance, Assessment and Impact of STEM Programs : Learning assessment, quality assurance, accreditation, impact studies. items.title: Ecocriticism and Representations of the Sea in the Arts in Portugal
items.location: Lisbon, Portugal · Alyse Kaweng Fan, Thales Reis Alecrim
items.excerpt: The sea and marine life are often considered as pillars of cultural heritage, identity, and collective memories in Portugal (Lourenço 2001; Lourenço 2014). From the historical milestones of the sixteenth century transoceanic navigation (Cortesão 1978) to literary production (Avelar 2024), the ocean has shaped the national imagination of Portugal (T...
items.fullDescription: The sea and marine life are often considered as pillars of cultural heritage, identity, and collective memories in Portugal (Lourenço 2001; Lourenço 2014). From the historical milestones of the sixteenth century transoceanic navigation (Cortesão 1978) to literary production (Avelar 2024), the ocean has shaped the national imagination of Portugal (Tomás 2013). As Antonio Barreto writes: "Heritage is a human creation and the results from history and culture. But there are natural realities that are transformed, through history, culture, and technology into heritage. Such is the case of the sea for the Portuguese" (2019: 15). As a form of heritage, this connection extends to gastronomy and, in a particularly profound way, to the musical sphere (Simões 2013: 45-62; Sardo 2013: 44-64). From fado, with its narratives of longing and loss, to the songs that gave rhythm to the the fishermen's daily work, the Portuguese musical repertoire has functioned as a living archive of human interaction with the environment. However, there is nothing natural or essentialist about this Portuguese relationship with the sea, for it is an imaginary that has been constructed and continously negotiated across historical periods, which also bears the imprint of colonialism (João 2015: 119-147; El-Shawan Castelo-Branco et al 2003: 73-142). More dramatically, the transformations driven by climate change and unsustainable industrial fishing in the twenty-first century threaten not only biodiversity (Ramos-Pereira 2004: 26-43), but also the cultural practices linked to it - a topic that still requires greater attention in schoarly research. In any case, beyond its material and economic dimensions, the sea in Portugal remains a space of imagination and transformation. As evoked in the songs of José Afonso, the sea is not just a site of identity and homecoming, but also a place of alterity and utopia. By singing of the sea, we evoke the possibility of a new era, in which culture and nature meet once again in a shared project of humanity - a vision that also resonates with the notion of the Blue Humanities (Mentz 2023; Oppermann 2019: 443-461). This call seeks to explore the intersection between what we have inherited and what we must protect. Therefore, we invite researchers, artists, and cultural practitioners to reflect on how these environmental changes reshape the meaning of the arts and their role as vehicles of identity and resistance. Papers are welcome on the following topics, amongst others: The sea as a cultural construct in Portuguese music and the arts; Music, critique, and resistance to climate crises in Portugal; Intersections between literature, music, and environmental heritage; Representations of the sea and music in literature and written culture; Music, sound, identity, and the politics of ocean memory; Collective memory, local identities, and soundscapes; The sea in film, the media, and popular culture; Folklore, folk groups, and the preservation of intangible cultural heritage; Coloniality and decoloniality of contemporary maritime identity; Transcultural and postcolonial perspectives on the maritime imaginary; Blue Humanities and music as an environmental archive; Resistance, utopia, and sound activism in the face of climate change; Tourism and heritage preservation of coastal musical cultures; Technology, marine sciences, and music; Ocean ethics, music, and maritime imaginaries; Phenomenology, aesthetics, listening, and maritime experience. Submission Guidelines Proposals should be sent to the email: cecc.fch@ucp.pt, and include paper title, A 300-word abstract in Portuguese or in English should be submitted for a 20-minute presentation, including a provisional title, a brief biographical note (max. 150 words) and contact details. Contributions may be in Portuguese or English. Abstract submission deadline: July 31, 2026 Notification of acceptance: September 15, 2026 After acceptance of abstracts, participants must register for the conference. Registration Fees and Deadlines The registration fee includes coffee breaks and conference materials. Early bird registration (September 16-30): €75 (student) | €100 (non-student) Regular registration (October 1-20): €100 (student) | €150 (non-student) Keynote Speakers Diana Gonçalves, Catholic University of Portugal Susana Sardo, University of Aveiro Organizers Alyse Kaweng Fan e Thales Reis Alecrim Scientific Committee Ana Matoso, CECC Ana Margarida Abrantes, CECC Carla Ganito, CECC Joana Moura, CECC Mário Avelar, CECC Nuno Amado, CECC Patrícia Anzini, CECC Pedro A. Mendes, INET-md Rita Bueno Maia, CECC Sofia Vieira Lopes, INET-md This activity is part of the 4ELEMENTS project, representing the element Water, funded by the Transform4Europe (T4EU) alliance, in a strategic collaboration between the Catholic University of Portugal, the University of Sofia (Bulgaria) and the University of Silesia (Poland).

items.fullDescription: CFP: Smell Studies Journal General Editors: Will Tullett, University of York and Manon Raffard, University of Manchester. Editorial board: Andrew Kettler, Chanelle Dupuis, Jayanthan Sriram, Ally Louks, Hsuan Hsu, Sandra Barré. Despite a proliferation of interdisciplinary grant-funded projects on smell, several international conferences dedicated to olfaction, extensive interest from within and outside academia, and a growing literature - the field of Smell Studies is not currently served by its own English-language journal. Smell Studies Journal will be the first international peer-reviewed journal devoted to the field of interdisciplinary Smell Studies. The journal's goal is to promote new research and foster intellectual exchange in the growing field of Smell Studies, which encompasses work across disciplines such as history, geography, anthropology, art history, literature, architecture, sociology, philosophy, environmental humanities, and practice-based arts and performance research. The journal, which will initially have one issue per year, will publish research articles, book reviews, as well as shorter opinion pieces and creative work. Research articles will be evaluated through an open peer review process by members of scientific boards and the editorial board. We would also seek to welcome special issues should such proposals be forthcoming. Pairing academic analysis and theoretical reading with the increased interest in smell and scent in public, the Smell Studies Journal aims to sustain the evolving community of scholars working in the field. Smell Studies Journal will focus specifically on olfaction as a critical object of inquiry, welcoming all approaches from across the humanities and humanistic social sciences. Unlike vision or sound, smell resists representation and archiving, raising methodological challenges that demand a dedicated space for scholarly debate. Olfaction is also irreducibly political: scholarship on smell has illuminated inequalities and injustice in ways that other sensory approaches have not. The journal welcomes contributions from across the humanities and humanistic social sciences, including but not limited to history, cultural history, literary studies, art history, aesthetics, cultural studies, environmental humanities, heritage studies, and curatorial and museum studies. While the journal does not exclude work from the natural or cognitive sciences, submissions from those fields are expected to engage substantively with humanistic frameworks and critical theory. The journal publishes peer-reviewed research articles, opinion essays and interviews, creative works, and reviews of books and exhibitions. We are currently planning the first few issues of the journal. These will appear in a prospectus to a leading academic press who have solicited a proposal for consideration. If we are successful in getting the journal a home then it is likely the first issue will appear in early 2028. As such, this is an initial call for articles, opinion essays, creative work, and reviews of books and exhibitions. We are accepting abstracts for research articles, and ideas for reviews, opinion pieces, creative work, and interviews. Specifically, for research articles please submit a title, 250-word abstract, and either a short biography or a link to where such a biography can be found online. For all other work, or to gauge interest in a research article prior to sending an abstract please e-mail smellstudiesjournal@gmail.com. Initial submissions of abstracts and initial inquiries for the first few issues are requested by the deadline of 1st of August 2026. Continued submissions beyond that date are also open for future issues and for general interest in Smell Studies Journal. Completed submissions for the first issue would be requested for January 1 of 2027, with reviews and editing in Spring and Summer of 2027.

items.fullDescription: Call for Chapters: Veterans Experiencing Homelessness, Education Equity, and the Technology Gap: Left Behind Editors Anna P. Archer, National University, United States Samira R. Galvao, National University, United States Call for Chapters Proposals Submission Deadline: August 5, 2026 Full Chapters Due: October 28, 2026 Submission Date: October 28, 2026 Introduction Each year, thousands of U.S. military veterans separate from service only to encounter housing instability, barriers to completing an education, and a widening gap in technology access. These are rarely treated as connected problems, but Left Behind starts from the premise that they are: interlinking failures of the systems meant to support the transition to civilian life. Drawing on data science and technology, public policy, sociology, education research, and health sciences, this volume examines how these failures take shape and what evidence-based responses look like, tracing how housing, education, and technology barriers reinforce one another, and where coordinated intervention can break that cycle. Organized across five thematic parts, the volume moves from problem definition through data infrastructure, higher-ed and technology-based interventions, and community and institutional responses, to concrete policy recommendations. Throughout, it maintains an explicit equity lens, centering veteran populations disproportionately affected but underrepresented in existing scholarship: women veterans, LGBTQ+ veterans, racial and ethnic minority veterans, and immigrant veterans navigating credential and language barriers. Objective Examine the intersecting crises of veteran homelessness, education inequity, and the technology gap through a multidisciplinary lens, drawing on data science and technology, public policy, sociology, education research, and health sciences. Provide a unified data science and technology, higher-education, and equity frameworks that integrate issues current scholarship treats in isolation (homelessness, underemployment, and technology access). Underrepresented veteran populations in existing literature: women veterans, LGBTQ+ veterans, racial and ethnic minority veterans, and immigrant veterans navigating credential and language barriers. Move from problem definition through data infrastructure, technology-based interventions, and community/institutional responses to concrete, evidence-based policy recommendations. Maintain an explicit equity lens throughout, modeling a standard for how the field discusses veterans experiencing homelessness. Target Audience Academic researchers in public policy, data science, education, sociology, social work, and health sciences; graduate and advanced undergraduate students in relevant disciplines; VA administrators, DoD policy staff, HUD officials, and federal and state policymakers; nonprofit and community-based organizations serving veteran populations; university veteran service programs and faculty in veteran-focused education; military and veteran advocacy organizations. Recommended Topics We are currently recruiting contributors for the following sections of the volume. Each section is self-contained; contributors should not assume cross-references to other chapters. AI and ML in Case Management for Veterans Experiencing Homelessness: AI tools for case worker support; automated triage; predictive resource allocation; current program examples. To show how AI-supported triage and resource allocation can extend the reach and effectiveness of case workers serving veterans experiencing homelessness. EdTech Solutions for Veteran Learners: LMS accessibility; adaptive learning platforms; online program design for nontraditional students; institutional adoption of EdTech for veteran students; faculty readiness; university-level implementation challenges. To evaluate which educational technologies and institutional adoption practices actually improve outcomes for nontraditional veteran students. Digital Literacy and Technology Access: Bridging basic tech access gaps before workforce entry; device and internet access; foundational skills training; public library as digital access point; information literacy and veteran populations. To address the foundational access and skills gaps, devices, connectivity, and basic digital literacy must be closed before any higher-level technology intervention can work for veterans. Higher Education's Role, Community & Institutional Responses: University veteran support centers; student veteran organizations; retention programs; institutional policy. To show how universities can build the institutional infrastructure, support centers, veteran organizations, retention programs, and policy that helps veteran students not just enroll, but stay, succeed, and remain connected to the resources that protect against housing instability. Psychological Impact of Military-to-Civilian Transition: Identity shift and loss of structure; belonging and isolation; psychological barriers to housing and education stability; adjustment disorders. To explain how identity disruption and loss of structure during transition create psychological vulnerability that can destabilize both housing and educational outcomes. Racial and Ethnic Minority Veterans: Intersecting Disparities: Compounding disadvantages; racial disparities in VA service access; cultural competency gaps; equity-centered interventions. To examine how racial and ethnic disparities compound the volume's core challenges, and to identify equity-centered interventions that address those compounding disadvantages directly. LGBTQ+ Veterans: Identity, Discrimination, and Barriers to Housing, Education, and Support Services: LGBTQ+ veteran identity and military service history; Don't Ask Don't Tell legacy and its ongoing impact; housing discrimination among LGBTQ+ veterans; campus climate and identity safety in higher ed; VA service gaps for LGBTQ+ veterans; intersectionality with race, gender, and immigration status. To document how identity-based discrimination and the legacy of Don't Ask Don't Tell continue to create housing, education, and service-access barriers unique to LGBTQ+ veterans. Mapping Veteran Data Ecosystems: VA, DoD, and HUD data systems; interoperability gaps; what data exists and what is missing; data sharing barriers. To inventory what veteran-related data exists across VA, DoD, and HUD systems, and expose the interoperability gaps that prevent coordinated, evidence-based responses. Coding Bootcamps, Credentialing, and Workforce Pipelines: Community college and university bootcamp partnerships; stackable credentials; degree pathways; ROI data. To assess how bootcamp and stackable-credential pathways can close the technology divide for veterans more quickly than traditional degree routes, and what ROI data supports that case. Faculty and Curriculum: Teaching Veterans in the Modern Classroom: Trauma-informed pedagogy; curriculum design for veteran learners; faculty training; classroom culture. To equip faculty with trauma-informed pedagogy and curriculum design practices that make classrooms genuinely accessible to veteran learners. Authors are also welcome to propose additional topics that fall within the scope of the book. Submission Procedure Researchers and practitioners are invited to submit on or before August 5, 2026, a chapter proposal of 1,000 to 2,000 words clearly explaining the mission and concerns of his or her proposed chapter. Authors will be notified by August 19, 2026 about the status of their proposals and sent chapter guidelines.Full chapters of a minimum of 10,000 words (word count includes references and related readings) are expected to be submitted by October 28, 2026, and all interested authors must consult the guidelines for manuscript submissions at https://www.igi-global.com/publish/contributor-resources/before-you-write/ prior to submission. All submitted chapters will be reviewed on a double-anonymized review basis. Contributors may also be requested to serve as reviewers for this project. Note: There are no submission or acceptance fees for manuscripts submitted to this book publication, Veterans Experiencing Homelessness, Education Equity, and the Technology Gap: Left Behind. All manuscripts are accepted based on a double-anonymized peer review editorial process. All proposals should be submitted through the eEditorial Discovery® online submission manager. Publisher This book is scheduled to be published by IGI Global Scientific Publishing, an international academic publisher of the "Information Science Reference", "Medical Information Science Reference", "Business Science Reference", and "Engineering Science Reference" imprints. IGI Global Scientific Publishing specializes in publishing reference books, scholarly journals, and electronic databases featuring academic research on a variety of innovative topic areas including, but not limited to, education, social science, medicine and healthcare, business and management, information science and technology, engineering, public administration, library and information science, media and communication studies, and environmental science. For additional information regarding the publisher, please visit https://www.igi-global.com. This publication is anticipated to be released in 2027. Indexing Information for Prospective Authors IGI Global Scientific Publishing meets the criteria for inclusion in major indexing services such as Scopus; however, it is important to note that all indexing decisions are made independently by these services. IGI Global Scientific Publishing books are selectively indexed by the indexing organization after publication. Indexing cannot be guaranteed for any book prior to publication, and the indexing organization has complete control over the final selection and timeline. Important Dates August 5, 2026: Proposal Submission Deadline August 19, 2026: Notification of Acceptance October 28, 2026: Full Chapter Submission December 9, 2026: Review Results Returned January 6, 2027: Final Acceptance Notification January 13, 2027: Final Chapter Submission Inquiries Anna P. Archer National University a.archer1909@student.nu.edu Samira R. Galvao National University sgalvao@nu.edu Classifications Computer Science and Information Technology; Education; Government and Law; Social Sciences and Humanities

items.title: CFP: ANATOMIES OF ANOMALY TRAJECTORIES OF DEFORMITY ACROSS LITERATURE, PERFORMANCE, AND CLINICAL DISCOURSE
items.excerpt: "[...]anomal ne signifie autre chose qu'insolite, inaccoutumé. On doit donc entendre d'une manière générale, par un être anomal, un être qui s'éloigne par son organisation de la grande majorité des êtres auxquels il doit être comparé[...]" (Isidore Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire, Histoire générale et particulière des anomalie...
items.fullDescription: "[...]anomal ne signifie autre chose qu'insolite, inaccoutumé. On doit donc entendre d'une manière générale, par un être anomal, un être qui s'éloigne par son organisation de la grande majorité des êtres auxquels il doit être comparé[...]" (Isidore Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire, Histoire générale et particulière des anomalies chez les hommes et les animaux , 1837) Conceived as the concluding conference of the PRIN project Tea-Med - Theatre and Medicine, Anatomies of Anomaly: Trajectories of Deformity across Literature, Performance, and Clinical Discourse aims to explore the category of the anomalous through its historical, cultural, medical, literary, and performative articulations. Drawing on the definition formulated by Isidore Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire - for whom the anomalous coincides with that which appears "unusual" or "uncommon," removed from the majority of bodies and forms deemed regular - the conference aims to expand this category to encompass everything that exceeds normative paradigms, escapes fixed classifications, and resists the systems of control to which it is subjected. In this sense, the anomalous does not merely designate bodily or physiological deformity, but also every form of alterity that destabilises the boundaries of the familiar: identities perceived as eccentric in relation to dominant models; female subjectivities that exceed or subvert historically imposed social and cultural roles; linguistic, narrative, and textual singularities; excluded or marginalised voices; experiences of madness, trauma, and disability; subversive literary and editorial practices; and bodies and minds that challenge the very notion of "normality". As Rosemarie Garland Thomson observes, "The unexpected body fires rich, if anxious, narratives and practices that probe the contours and boundaries of what we take to be human" (Freakery, 1). The conference therefore aims to interrogate those forms of representation, narration, and discourse that have contributed - and continue to contribute - to destabilising and reshaping the category of the human itself; discourses, cultural practices, and modes of knowledge that challenge the boundaries of the representable, the intelligible, and the normative. From this perspective, the conference seeks to foster an interdisciplinary dialogue among scholars working in the history of medicine, literary and linguistic studies, theatre and performance studies, disability studies, gender studies, queer studies, philosophy, psychoanalysis, cultural history, visual arts, and related fields. Particular attention will be devoted to theatre as a privileged site for the exhibition, negotiation, and representation of anomaly: a space in which the deformed, pathological, eccentric, or marginal body is observed, medicalised, spectacularised, or rewritten through new narrative and political possibilities. Possible topics include, but are not limited to: • The monstrous body: teratology, eugenics, and studies of physical alterity • Anomalous minds: madness, trauma, and psycho-physical pathologies • Disability as anomaly: historical, social, and medical representations of disability • Gendered anomaly: queer bodies, performativity, and non-normative identities • Anomaly as social role: disguise, cross-dressing, and the subversion of gender norms • Spectacles of anomaly: freak shows, circuses, and fairs • Anomalous language: linguistic, narrative, dramaturgical, and textual anomalies • The anomalous as archive: case studies, medical documentation, archival gaps, and recording practices • The anomalous as a tool of social control: medicalisation, marginalisation, and the disciplining of bodies • Disturbing anomalies: the grotesque, the pathological, and the uncanny • Anomalous performances: voice, gesture, stage presence, and eccentric corporeality • The anomalous in visual and media cultures: visual arts, cinema, media, and performance • Anomalous spaces: ecologies, environments, and landscapes of deformity • Spiritual anomalies: possession, ecstasy, and altered states • The anomalous beyond the human: posthumanism, alterity, and the redefinition of the norm • Anomalous societies: non-Eurocentric cultural practices and social structures Submission Guidelines The conference welcomes contributions spanning all historical periods and geographical contexts, with a particular emphasis on comparative and interdisciplinary approaches. The call for papers is open to PhD students and early career researchers interested in contributing to the conference discussion. Proposals, in Italian, English, or French, must be submitted by 15 August 2026 to convegnoanomalie.unifi@gmail.com. Submissions should be sent as a .pdf or .docx file named "Surname_AnatomiesConference" and must include the title, an abstract (maximum 300 words), 5 keywords, 5-7 bibliographical references relevant to the proposed topic and a short bio-bibliographical note (maximum 150 words). Authors are also required to specify their institutional affiliation, disciplinary field, and the language of their presentation (Italian, English, or French). All proposals will be evaluated by the Scientific Committee. Notification of acceptance will be communicated by 30 September 2026. Each speaker will have 20 minutes for their presentation. The conference will take place in person on Thursday 26 and Friday 27 November 2026 at the University of Florence. Further details regarding the venue and conference programme will be provided upon confirmation of participation. Participation in the conference is free of charge and accommodation expenses for one night will be reimbursed. Participants are responsible for their own meals and travel costs.

items.title: Inviting Interdisciplinarity: Engaging Students through Multimodal Fantasy and Sci-Fi
items.location: Atlanta, GA · South Atlantic Modern Language Association
items.excerpt: This panel explores how multimodal science fiction and fantasy pedagogies invites student engagement, retention, and career readiness across disciplines. Building on research demonstrating that storytelling supports the retention and transfer of knowledge, the panel argues that speculative and multimodal teaching practices create immersive learning enviro...
items.fullDescription: This panel explores how multimodal science fiction and fantasy pedagogies invites student engagement, retention, and career readiness across disciplines. Building on research demonstrating that storytelling supports the retention and transfer of knowledge, the panel argues that speculative and multimodal teaching practices create immersive learning environments that connect imagination to real-world application. Through narrative analysis, creative production, digital storytelling, and multimodal assignments, science fiction and fantasy invite students to critically engage with contemporary issues such as artificial intelligence, ethics, climate futures, technological change, and social justice while developing transferable communication and analytical skills. Panelists will demonstrate how speculative pedagogies host meaningful intersections between the humanities and STEM fields by encouraging interdisciplinary thinking, ethical reflection, and collaborative problem solving. In doing so, the session advocates for the continued necessity of the humanities as spaces for cultivating critical leadership, creativity, and public engagement in technologically integrated futures. The panel also positions multimodal science fiction and fantasy as uniquely inclusive teaching frameworks that invite diverse student populations into academic inquiry through accessible and culturally resonant forms of storytelling. In addition to theoretical and pedagogical discussions, panelists will share practical classroom approaches, multimodal assignments, and teaching resources that attendees can adapt for their own courses. Ultimately, the panel argues that speculative and multimodal pedagogies move students from the imagined to the real by transforming creative engagement into critical thinking and professional practice. Please submit proposals for the panel with a title, 250-word abstract and a short 50-word bio to Dr. Lainie Pomerleau (lpomerleau@ccga.edu) and Dr. Zita Hüsing zhusing@uttyler.edu by August 15, 2026. SAMLA 2026 takes place November 5-7, 2026 in Atlanta, GA.

items.title: Embodying the WPA: Advice Narratives for Writing Program Administration
items.excerpt: Embodying the WPA: Advice Narratives for Writing Program Administration Edited by Jackie Hoermann-Elliott, Juliette Holder, Jennifer Judd, & Danielle Littlefield Brady Even among the quietest of us, there are stories to be told. Stories of how we dressed for the campus visit, whether or not we drank wine with the search committee at dinner. Stories from t...
items.fullDescription: Embodying the WPA: Advice Narratives for Writing Program Administration Edited by Jackie Hoermann-Elliott, Juliette Holder, Jennifer Judd, & Danielle Littlefield Brady Even among the quietest of us, there are stories to be told. Stories of how we dressed for the campus visit, whether or not we drank wine with the search committee at dinner. Stories from the first year as the WPA, remembering how we physically composed (or contorted) ourselves and our offices for comfort - our own or others'. Stories of how we hugged a bereaved teacher, toasted in celebration, or laughed a little too loud at that one department meeting. All WPAs have stories, deeply embodied tales whispered over coffee at conferences to colleagues or soon-to-be WPAs, and this edited collection seeks to gather many of those essential stories into one book that will benefit early-career and graduate WPAs for years to come. As seen in the recent call for Learning on the Bones: (Un)Becoming Writing Program Administrators (edited by Casie Fedukovich, Joyce Inman, Paula Patch, and Sherry Rankins-Robertson), there's a groundswell of scholarly interest in WPA physical and mental wellbeing, particularly for seasoned WPAs considering how to transition to new roles outside of writing program administration. Embodying the WPA differs in its emphasis on the physically material and/or visceral experiences of new WPAs, broadly and inclusively defined to include graduate WPAs, early-career WPAs, or quite possibly faculty returning to WPA work after a long tenure away. Questions to Consider Proposals should prompt readers to explore questions, such as: Whose bodies are (un)welcome in writing programs? How can embodied practices shape writing programs? When do our bodies get in the way of our WPA work, and how do we react? When and how do we rely on our bodies to support our work? Or others' work? Where do our material/physical positionalities and locations reside in relation to others? In what ways has WPA scholarship tended to focus on disembodied rather than embodied dimensions of WPA work How can candid discussions of embodied experiences benefit the next generation of WPAs When have embodied experiences made WPA labor feel joyous or rewarding? These questions are intended to encourage creative thinking around this topic. Contributors might also think in terms of relevant keywords, including but not limited to: racism, sexism, ableism, homophobia, or religious discrimination; dressing and officing practices; chronic illness and disability; child-free, pregnancy, or parenting status; mindfulness, exercise, or wellbeing; creativity and hobbies; fun, playfulness, joy, or connection; grief, trauma, and crisis; mindfulness or contemplative practices; public speaking and physical presence; exhaustion and burnout; and celebration or commemoration. While the focus of this collection is on the experiences of and advice for new WPAs, we welcome submissions from WPAs of all ranks (including graduate students) because our goal is to include narratives from a range of perspectives, voices, and institutional contexts. Advice Narrative Genre Embodying the WPA also differs from other books in terms of form while leveraging what many WPAs do best: give great advice. The genre for chapters will mirror the style of short advice narratives (2,500-4,000 words) published in Parenting While PhDing (Rutgers UP, 2025), which are brief and accessible to the busy administrator and make space for a range of diverse perspectives and storytelling approaches. Successful proposals will feature: an evergreen or timeless, tagline piece of advice in the title, author name(s), email address(es), institutional affiliation(s), a description of the narrative (up to 500 words) that connects to the title, integration of scholarly sources is encouraged, but not required Submission & Contact Information Questions can be sent to embodyingthewpa@gmail.com, and proposals can be sent to the same address no later than 11:59 pm on Friday, August 21st, 2026. The anticipated timeline for the collection is as follows: August 21, 2026: Proposal submissions due October 2, 2026: Acceptances sent January 8, 2026: Complete submissions due March 5, 2027: Feedback provided May 14, 2027: Revised submissions due

items.title: Engaging Research Across Humanities (ERAH) Graduate Conference
items.excerpt: Date: October 10-11, 2026 Location: SMU Main Campus, Dallas, TX Theme: Movement & Borderlands Abstract Submission Deadline: August 28, 2026 Keynotes: Dr. Tim Bowman & Dr. Elda María Román Graduate students of Southern Methodist University's Departments of English, History, and Anthropology, in collaboration with the Moody School of Advanced...
items.fullDescription: Date: October 10-11, 2026 Location: SMU Main Campus, Dallas, TX Theme: Movement & Borderlands Abstract Submission Deadline: August 28, 2026 Keynotes: Dr. Tim Bowman & Dr. Elda María Román Graduate students of Southern Methodist University's Departments of English, History, and Anthropology, in collaboration with the Moody School of Advanced Studies, Dedman College Interdisciplinary Institute, and the department of English, have collaborated to hold the third annual Engaging Research Across the Humanities (ERAH) Conference at SMU from October 10-October 11, 2026. ERAH 2026 will prioritize the development of interdisciplinary connections and aims to foster a supportive environment for both graduate students and advanced undergraduates engaged in strong research. There will be special emphasis placed on creating opportunities for participants with limited or no prior conference experience. The theme for ERAH 2026 is Movement & Borderlands. If you have any questions, please email arundhatig@smu.edu. Borders simultaneously construct and limit meaning. By demarcating entities, we hold the power to define space and identity. In the same manner, those entities are restricted to oppositional definitions. What does it mean to occupy borderlands, to move across them and through them? How is the deconstruction of borders, or reconceptualization of definitional demarcation, itself an act of meaning-making that exists without opposition? How do borderlands complicate cultural conceptions rooted in duality? How does movement across and through borderlands produce opportunity for new forms of meaning-making not rooted in hard lines and limitations? This year's theme asks us to think critically about the roles of both movement and borderlands in our respective disciplines by considering these questions and others. We invite you to consider the roles of both movement and borderlands within the works you study-be it literary works, historical archives, etc.-as well as how pervasive disciplinary paradigms shape the ways in which you approach your scholarship. Possible topics may include but are not limited to: Migration, mobility, and displacement Citizenship and belonging Racial, ethnic, national, religious identity Borders as constructs Frontiers Artistic and embodied movement Indigenous perspectives on imposed borders Nationalism and borderlands Environmental and ecological borders Linguistic borders Tourism and global circulation We invite abstracts of 200-250 words for both individual presentations and fully-constructed panels (3-4 speakers). Papers in STEM fields that clearly articulate relevance to the theme are welcome. Full-panel proposals will be given priority. Panel proposals must include a 200-250 word proposal for the panel in addition to abstracts for each individual participant. A select number of travel grants will be awarded on a competitive basis for those traveling more than 100 miles. To qualify for a travel grant, you must submit your application by August 14, 2026. Further details will be included in your registration sign-up. Please submit abstracts via the following google form link: https://tinyurl.com/2026ERAH

items.title: NeMLA Panel 2027: Women's Writing and the Question of Canon
items.excerpt: Women's writing emerged as a critical category in response to the historical exclusion of women from literary canons and archives. The recovery of neglected texts, authors, and traditions has transformed literary studies, creating new genealogies of reading and interpretation. Yet the category itself raises important questions. Does the designation "women...
items.fullDescription: Women's writing emerged as a critical category in response to the historical exclusion of women from literary canons and archives. The recovery of neglected texts, authors, and traditions has transformed literary studies, creating new genealogies of reading and interpretation. Yet the category itself raises important questions. Does the designation "women's writing" continue to serve as a productive framework, or does it risk reinforcing the very marginalization it seeks to address by positioning women outside the literary mainstream? As Elaine Showalter argues, feminist criticism should not simply "fit women between the lines of the male tradition" but attend to the cultural and historical conditions that shape women's literary production and reception. Her influential formulation of gynocriticism intends to recover neglected women writers and establish women's writing as a significant field of scholarly inquiry. Subsequent feminist scholarship has complicated these foundations further. Black feminist critics such as Barbara Smith, bell hooks, Hortense Spillers challenge the tendency to treat "woman" as a universal category, while postcolonial theorists including Gayatri Chakravorty Spivak and Chandra Talpade Mohanty question the assumption of a coherent global female experience. Likewise, theorists such as Judith Butler and Jack Halberstam have interrogated the stability of gender itself. Together, these interventions have encouraged scholars to frame women's writing not as a singular tradition but as a diverse and contested field shaped by race, class, caste, sexuality, nationality, language, and other intersecting forms of identity and power. This panel invites papers that reconsider critical strategies for studying women's writing, with particular attention to the reception of women's writing. How do questions of race, class, sexuality, language, nation, and geography influence the ways women writers are read, taught, circulated, translated, archived, and canonized? Topics may include feminist literary criticism, reception studies, archival practices, translation, publishing histories, readerships, pedagogy, and comparative perspectives on women's writing across languages and cultures.

items.title: Déplacements dans les réseaux transcoloniaux : l'Asie, l’Afrique et/ou l’Europe reliés
items.excerpt: Pendant la période de l'Indochine française, les premiers vietnamiens sont acheminés en Nouvelle-Calédonie en tant que travailleurs sous-contrat dans les mines de nickel. Cette période marque en parallèle la rébellion très présente des autochtones de l'île - les Kanak - face à la dépossession territoriale des colons français. Le...
items.fullDescription: Pendant la période de l'Indochine française, les premiers vietnamiens sont acheminés en Nouvelle-Calédonie en tant que travailleurs sous-contrat dans les mines de nickel. Cette période marque en parallèle la rébellion très présente des autochtones de l'île - les Kanak - face à la dépossession territoriale des colons français. Le refus de participer à l'industrie minière d'un groupe favorise l'expatriation de l'autre sur l'archipel en pleine ère de la colonisation française dans plusieurs colonies. Le roi tonkinois Ham Nghi, à l'âge de 18 ans, a été placé en exil en Algérie après des années de mouvements de résistance contre la puissance coloniale française. Son expulsion fait écho à celle de la Reine malgache Ranavalona III, qui a eu lieu après l'annexion de Madagascar par la France en 1896. L'ensemble de l'empire français, étendu de l'Indochine à l'Afrique insulaire, est sujet à la déportation dans la plus grande colonie de peuplement. Ce réseau de déportation des colonies liées met en avant la question des outils de domination employés par le système colonial. Malgré les différentes populations colonisées, la stratégie de domination s'applique d'une certaine façon. **** Dans le cadre académique au sein des études francophones et françaises, nous voyons souvent des pistes de spécialisation distinctes, telles que les études européennes, les études caribéennes, et les études postcoloniales francophones. Les cours d'histoire et de littérature offrent également des réflexions particulières selon une région spécifique ainsi qu'une époque donnée. L'objectif de former les futurs chercheurs dans des domaines précis, faute d'études approfondies sur les caractéristiques transcontinentales, risque de faire oublier qu'au coeur de l'histoire de l'un est tissée celle de l'autre. Cette imbrication transnationale et transcommunautaire, inévitable soit-elle, est due à la fois aux déplacements migratoires, à l'exil d'après-guerre, et à la mobilisation de la force de travail par le système capitaliste et colonial. Lorsque l'on met en évidence ce lien intrinsèque entre des événements, on fait émerger un réseau transcolonial qui lie l'Afrique, l'Asie, et/ou l'Europe. Ce réseau nous oblige à réfléchir à l'interconnexion de l'un à l'autre, d'une communauté spécifique à une autre soi-disant éloignée. Dans le contexte du monde d'aujourd'hui, une telle conscience de l'interconnexion des histoires coloniales peut susciter des réflexions : Peut-on vraiment ignorer le combat de l'autre ? Quels seront les enjeux ? Communications à envoyer soit en anglais, soit en français.

The journal publishes scholarly texts providing scientific reflections in the field of media and communication studies, which presents the current development of the media environment in the context of visual media and art. EJMAP reflects on the theoretical and visual aspects of media communication, which includes media art, visual arts, photography, film, literature and advertising, as well as current topics discussed in the field of photography, photography theory, advertising theory, theatrical art and visual theories related to image interpretation. Topics of interest include, but are not limited to, the following: · current trends in the media environment; · development in the context of visual media and art; · theoretical and visual aspects of media communication (including media art, visual arts, photography, film, literature, and advertising); · topics discussed in the field of photography, photography theory, and advertising theory; · image interpretation of media communication and art forms. Contributions: · theoretical articles; · theoretical articles combined with presentations of research results; · research results including their practical implementation; · art portfolios; · reviews of works of art; · interviews; · reports of current events in the journal's field of expertise; · other publications, shorter news articles, and essays. The scope of analysis is wide-ranging, encompassing literature and culture, language studies and a...
items.fullDescription: Call for Chapters Rhetoric and Communication. Interdisciplinary Studies in the Humanities The intersection of Rhetoric and Communication continues to attract the interest of many scholars, particularly within the fields of the Humanities and Social Sciences. The scope of analysis is wide-ranging, encompassing literature and culture, language studies and advertising, communication studies and politics, among other domains. In this Volume, we aim to bring together a set of studies that focus directly on topics shared by Rhetoric and Communication, from Classical Antiquity to the present, from an interdisciplinary perspective. Scholarly contributions across the Humanities will enhance our understanding of the influence of rhetoric and communication on society, taking into account, on the one hand, there is a vast legacy that merits further study and, on the other, new forms of communication require us to reassess the impact of the rhetorical art. This Volume welcomes contributions of a theoretical or empirical nature, including case studies, from within the field of the Humanities. Possible topics include: · Rhetorical and communication theory applied to discourse; · Treatises on rhetoric and communication; · The presence of rhetorical genres in contemporary forms of communication; · Argumentation techniques and persuasion in literary texts, the media, advertising, or other narrative forms; · The contemporary relevance of teaching rhetoric and communication; · The contemporary application of classical rhetoric in the media; · Rhetoric in political discourse and in literary composition; · Philosophical perspectives on rhetoric and communication; · The role of the audience and the addressee in rhetorical theory and in the communicative process; · Representations of rhetoric and communication in the Arts. Editors: Cristina Santos Pinheiro, Joaquim Pinheiro, Rui Carlos Fonseca, Samuel Mateus, Telmo Reis e Vanessa Cesário Proposals, in Word format, should be submitted by 10 September 2026 to the following email address: retorica.comunicacao@mail.uma.pt The Volume is scheduled for publication in March 2027 by a leading national publisher. All submissions will be subject to scientific evaluation (blind peer review). Authors will be notified of the review decision by 30 November 2026. 1. Accepted languages Submissions will be accepted in Portuguese, French, English, and Spanish. 2. Formatting guidelines 2.1. Length and formatting: main text: maximum of 20 A4 pages and minimum of 12 A4 pages; font: 11 pt, Aptos, 1.5 line spacing; footnotes: 10 pt, Aptos, single spacing. 2.2. Title centred, 12 pt, bold, Aptos. 2.3. Author name(s), affiliation (University, Institute, Research Unit or equivalent), email address, and ORCID should be provided in 11 pt, Aptos. 2.4. Two abstracts (maximum 250 words each) must be included: one in the language of the article and one in English, followed by up to five keywords. 3. General guidelines 3.1. Citations and references should follow APA style (7th ed.); all citations must be properly identified. 3.2. Quotations longer than three lines must be indented 1 cm from the left margin. 3.3. a) Italics should be used for the titles of works, modern monographs, and journals. b) Quotation marks (" ") should be used for quotations from modern texts. c) Italics should not be used for Latin abbreviations (op. cit., loc. cit., cf., ibid., in…). 4. Footnotes Footnotes should be brief. 5. Images/Graphs/Tables 5.1. Any graphical elements must be clearly identified and numbered. 5.2. Images must be submitted in .jpeg format, with a minimum resolution of 300 dpi, be free of copyright fees, and accompanied by official proof of permission or purchase of rights for academic publication. Each submission may include no more than four images (printing will not be in color). 6. Final bibliography The bibliography should be limited to essential works or cited references and must conform to APA style (7th ed.). 7. Publication The Volume will be published by a Portuguese publisher with extensive experience in scientific publishing. It will also be made available as an e-book in institutional repositories. 8. Copyright statement Submissions must be original, and authors grant publication rights upon acceptance.

items.location: V&A East Storehouse · British Association for American Studies
items.excerpt: Transatlantic Connections and the 'Special Relationship[s]': BAAS Postgraduate Symposium, 2026 V&A East Storehouse, London 9th November In 1946, Winston Churchill declared the need for a "special relationship between the British Commonwealth and Empire and the United States" in order to maintain world diplomacy, citing our shared language and cultural val...
items.fullDescription: Transatlantic Connections and the 'Special Relationship[s]': BAAS Postgraduate Symposium, 2026 V&A East Storehouse, London 9th November In 1946, Winston Churchill declared the need for a "special relationship between the British Commonwealth and Empire and the United States" in order to maintain world diplomacy, citing our shared language and cultural values as connecting the two countries. Although this 'special relationship' has continually been called into question by scholars, there is undoubtedly a channel of cultural and political exchange between the US and the UK, as well as with other countries, which has become even more urgent for discussion in recent years. The 2026 BAAS Postgraduate Symposium invites proposals from postgraduate researchers at all levels and across all disciplines on the topic of Transatlantic Connections and the 'Special Relationship[s]'. By using the term 'Special Relationship[s]', we intend to explore the relationship between not only the US and the UK, but also between the US and other countries. We invite panels and individual papers on this theme and encourage you to interpret it as broadly as you wish, including any aspect of the US's transnational cultural, political and literary connections, influences or contrasts. We welcome contributions from all realms of American Studies, including, but not limited to: literature and film studies, postcolonial and decolonial analysis, science and visual culture, and urban and environmental history. As this year's symposium will be held at V&A East Storehouse, the site of the David Bowie Archive, we are particularly keen on hearing from scholars exploring the etymologically and culturally rich prefix of the term 'Transatlantic' itself. This one-day symposium will involve not only academic panel discussions, but other activities such as a career development session, an introduction to the archives at the Victoria and Albert Museum, and a keynote address by Professor Mark Ford. The intention is to foster a strong postgraduate community in American Studies and to learn from both our peers and from leaders in our field. The symposium will be catered, hosted primarily in-person at V&A East Storehouse, but with a digital attendance option for those only able to join online. There will be time in the day to explore V&A East Storehouse and the David Bowie Centre. V&A East Storehouse is a working museum store with a rich archive for students to use in their research. The building is designed to allow visitors to get closer to objects held in the V&A collections and to the work of the museum itself. We welcome the submission of individual papers as well as proposals for complete panels of three researchers. Applications from groups of three or four researchers for roundtable discussions are also welcome. For panel and roundtable proposals, please also include a statement of up to 200 words on the aims of the collective submission, alongside the individual papers. Papers should be around 15-20 minutes long , and roundtables will consist of five-minute introductions by each researcher followed by 45 minutes of discussion. Interdisciplinary paper, panel and roundtable proposals are encouraged. Suggested paper and roundtable topics include, but are not limited to: - Case studies or broader comparisons between literary texts from the US and other nations - Case studies or broader comparisons between films from the US and other nations - Comparative political or historical analyses concerning the US and other nations - Comparative economic, scientific or environmental analyses concerning the US and other nations. Although the symposium will centre on 'Transatlantic Connections', we welcome proposals making any transnational comparisons rooted in American Studies. We especially encourage researchers from across Europe and beyond to submit and attend, as part of ongoing efforts to expand the EAAS community of postgraduates. We want to make this symposium as accessible as possible; we will be working to ensure a relaxed academic environment and we encourage anyone attending to email us with any access needs. Bursaries will be available from BAAS on a limited basis for students requiring financial support for attendance. The deadline for submissions is? 15th September 2026 .?Please send proposals including a title, an abstract of no more than? 250 words ?and?a short biography of no more than 200 words ?to Claire Watt at?claire.watt@baas.ac.uk. If you have any questions about the symposium, big or small, please get in touch and we will be happy to help. We look forward to hearing from you!

items.title: In and Out of Media – Intermediality in the Humanities of Today
items.excerpt: Historically, researching and teaching the different art forms took place in a well-sealed vacuum: for example, using literary theory to decipher one of the great texts, visual and compositional analysis to examine a sublime painting, or montage splicing to perceive the nuances of film. However, it is becoming more difficult to recognize the borders that...
items.fullDescription: Historically, researching and teaching the different art forms took place in a well-sealed vacuum: for example, using literary theory to decipher one of the great texts, visual and compositional analysis to examine a sublime painting, or montage splicing to perceive the nuances of film. However, it is becoming more difficult to recognize the borders that separate cinema, literature, and the visual arts. Factors including the rise of globalized societies and the emergence of new technology have forced us to reconsider medium specificity and our relationship with creative expressions and works of art. As scholars and educators, the current moment compels us to reevaluate our toolbox and to reconceive the humanities more holistically - in and out of its different media. To this end, intermedial approaches have emerged, drawing their meaning from the "impure" circumstances to which they belong. Hence, as a conceptual approach, intermediality stands to both preserve and transform what was once understood as a "print-based public sphere." How can the concept help us reconceive notions like the commons, the public sphere and civil society in the humanities classrooms of today? How does a scrutiny of the relationships between creative media help nurture the critical skills needed to participate in an ever more dispersed and multimodal civil society? How can interpreting a film as poetic or a brush stroke as literary inform our civic engagement and our research? These are the types of questions we look forward to in this roundtable discussion. All disciplines and time periods are welcome. Abstracts must be submitted through NeMLA portal. Deadline: Sept. 30, 2026. Questions? Contact jchavez@brynmawr.edu and jmesi09@sas.upenn.edu

items.fullDescription: "Reconsidering War and Cinema: A Contemporary Reflection" provides a wide panorama to revisit and reflect on the continuing interest of themes relating to war and global cinema. Four areas are of especial interest: careful analyses of short video clips of films about war; the connection between film and fiction; enduring patterns of representation of war in cinema; international comparisons. We welcome presentations on films from traditions other than that of major US studios. References to the relationship between historic cinema and contemporary global experience of national or internal bellicosity are certainly welcome.

items.fullDescription: Sponsored by the Association for the Study of Literature and the Environment (ASLE) This roundtable envisions writing as a collaborative act and centers writings that empower Environmental Justice communities. How have/do writing communities negotiate and enact environmental justice? How do writing communities disseminate scientific and technical information towards environmental action and justice? Participants in this roundtable are encouraged to consider: o How does/can writing raise collective environmental consciousness? o Writing in the Humanities to mobilize marginalized communities in the age of climate change o Aesthetic writings beyond the pleasure of nature writing but those that fuel ethical actions o Place-based writing that also takes into account displacement, deracination, and forced climate migration o Writing that argues for multispecies kinship and justice o Writing that transcends linguistic structures and includes other material realities o Transdisciplinary rhetorical writing that transforms/translates scientific communication and empowers citizens o Writing as a way to organize collective environmental action in the face of systemic injustice. Format: roundtable (5-10 participants) Please submit a 200-300-word abstract ONLY via the NeMLA portal by 9/30/2026. Email: Dr. Gayathri Goel goelg@bc.edu

items.fullDescription: Artificial Intelligence is reshaping how teaching and learning take place across educational contexts. This seminar explores innovative and evidence-based approaches to integrating AI into classroom practice, language education, skills development, and assessment. We invite proposals that examine practical applications of AI in teaching and learning, including but not limited to: • AI-enhanced teaching practices and instructional innovation • Generative AI in language education and English language teaching • AI-supported speaking, writing, reading, and listening development • AI and academic writing instruction • AI-assisted feedback and formative assessment • Assessment design in the era of AI • AI for critical thinking, creativity, and problem-solving • AI and self-regulated learning • AI-powered tutoring and personalized learning • Communicative language teaching and AI integration • AI for multilingual and intercultural learning • Student experiences with AI-enhanced learning • Teacher readiness and professional development • Classroom-based research and pedagogical case studies • Best practices for responsible AI use in educational settings We welcome empirical research, classroom-based studies, conceptual papers, pedagogical innovations, and practitioner reflections from scholars and educators across disciplines. The seminar aims to showcase how AI can be meaningfully integrated into teaching and learning to support student success and educational innovation. Submission Information Conference: Northeast Modern Language Association (NeMLA) Annual Convention 2027 Seminar: AI-Enhanced Teaching and Learning Abstract Length: 200-300 words Proposal Deadline: September 30, 2026 Conference Dates: March 6-9, 2027 Conference Venue: Salve Regina University, Newport, Rhode Island, USA Please submit your proposal via the official NeMLA panel session listing page:

items.fullDescription: The 1995 film Smoke, directed by Wayne Wang, features characters who band together by frequenting a corner cigar store in Brooklyn. Paul Auster, who wrote the screenplay, once described the community his characters created as a "little universe for themselves that is not determined by blood or the past." Members of traditional, fixed communities often belong because of generational roots and longtime residency. This panel session will explore smaller, and often more liminal, communities that are forged through other means. In such communities, membership is not dependent on family ties or connection to place. This community is one of commonality: members come together through common needs, interests, traumas, or goals. They may be temporary or long lasting, healthy or dysfunctional, organized or informal. Questions to consider might include, but are not limited to, the following: Under what circumstances do these "little universe" communities form, and what is their function? How do they fit (or not fit) into the larger community, and how do they reflect or perhaps indict the larger community? How do they transform the lives of their members? What unexpected effects do the groups have on their members? What happens when these communities disband? Possible examples of these communities: Support groups Virtual communities Groups that form in institutions (colleges, prisons) Clubs and groups of people with similar interests (book clubs, game-playing clubs, hiking groups, knitting circles, etc.) Communities formed of exclusion/communities of outsiders This panel welcomes papers that examine prose fiction, poetry, film, documentary, nonfiction, and culture. Paper proposals are accepted through September 30, 2026. Abstracts are 200-300 words in length, and titles should not exceed 100 characters. Please submit your abstract at the session's portal: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22409 Modality: This session is in hybrid format (in person, but will accept remote presentations). For more information on the convention and to view NeMLA's guidelines for abstracts, visit https://www.nemla.org Questions regarding the panel may be addressed to Lori Cummings at LoNewco1@wsc.edu
