items.fullDescription: Colloque international Cinéma libanais histoires, enjeux et patrimoines cinématographiques Caen - 12-13-14 avril 2027 En 1897, Alexandre Promio, opérateur pour les frères Lumières, pose son cinématographe à Beyrouth. Sur la place des canons (Sahet El Bourj), Promio filme ce qui restera comme la plus ancienne trace animée de la ville. Cette « vue lumière » n'est pas seulement le premier jalon technique du cinéma au Liban ; elle est l'acte de naissance du regard cinématographique sur un territoire qui, pendant 130 ans, ne cessera d'être filmé, modelé et reconstruit par les images. A deux ans du centenaire officiel du cinéma national, marqué par Les Aventures d'Elias Mabrouk (Giordano Pidutti, 1929), ce colloque se veut être une étape préparatoire de réflexion. Entre ce premier film de l'italien Giordano Pidutti et l'échéance symbolique de 2029, le cinéma libanais aurait traversé des cycles de création, d'interruption, de destruction et de renaissance. Comment penser cette histoire lorsque ses premières images ont été produites depuis un regard extérieur, dans un contexte où les conditions de production, de diffusion et de réception du cinéma étaient déjà étroitement liées à des enjeux politiques coloniaux ? Se réunir en 2027 permet de poser les fondements d'une réflexion critique sur ce siècle d'images avant les célébrations officielles, dans un moment où la question de la mémoire nationale est plus brûlante que jamais. Organiser ce colloque répond à un besoin de comprendre les bouleversements artistiques, culturels et politiques contemporains au Liban. Comme Promio qui, en 1897, a saisi le mouvement à Beyrouth, nous cherchons aujourd'hui à saisir les mouvements cinématographiques qui, malgré l'instabilité persistante, refusent de s'éteindre. Notre colloque s'articulera autour de quatre axes complémentaires : I) Le cinéma national et international : Filmer Beyrouth « Beyrouth a longtemps joué le rôle d'orientale favorite de l'Occident. Une favorite sans rivale au Proche-Orient. Ville de toutes les cultures, de toutes les affaires, de tous les plaisirs, mais aussi ville de tous les drames, Beyrouth sera la ville de tous les mythes ». C'est avec ces mots que Jocelyne Saab et Roland-Pierre Paringaux présentent la capitale libanaise dans leur projet Beyrouth, mille et une images. Ce projet est né de la volonté de créer une Cinémathèque à Beyrouth[1] en regroupant les films occidentaux et orientaux qui ont façonné l'image de la ville. Saab et Paringaux ont programmé en 1993 à la Cinémathèque française et à l'Institut du monde arabe une cinquantaine de films tournés à Beyrouth. En suivant leurs pas, ce premier axe propose d'étudier les images de Beyrouth. Il s'agit d'analyser comment la ville, dépourvue d'identité stable et invariable, devient, à travers les regards des cinéastes, un espace hétéroclite façonné par les affects, plutôt qu'un terrain géographique neutre. Du « simulacre » produit par le cinéma international, pour reprendre les mots du chercheur Elie Yazbek[2], à la « subjectivisation » opérée par le cinéma libanais, Beyrouth apparaît comme une ville-fantôme, sans cesse réinventée par l'objectif. Pour ouvrir la réflexion et guider les futur·e·s intervenant·e·s, cet axe propose quelques pistes de réflexion non exhaustives : · L'évolution de l'image de Beyrouth dans le cinéma · Analyse comparative des méthodes de représentation (cinéma occidental / cinéma libanais, fiction / documentaire, cinéma libanais / cinéma au Liban) · Fragmentation urbaine et cartographie d'une ville sans repères : espace labyrinthique II) Histoire du Liban et Histoire du cinéma : enjeux d'écritures et de représentations Cet axe de recherche examine la relation entre la production filmique et le récit historique au Liban à travers deux approches complémentaires : l'écriture cinématographique de l'histoire du pays et l'écriture de l'histoire du cinéma libanais. Dans un contexte où l'enseignement de l'histoire nationale présente des lacunes et s'arrête officiellement à l'indépendance de 1943, le cinéma occupe une fonction de représentation essentielle. Il permet de documenter une histoire contemporaine souvent absente des programmes scolaires, devenant ainsi un support pédagogique et social capable de proposer un récit là où le manuel fait défaut. Cette démarche s'inscrit dans les réflexions actuelles, notamment portées par l'Association libanaise d'histoire (ALH), sur la nécessité de former les jeunes générations à l'analyse critique des sources. En apprenant à distinguer l'information de son interprétation tout en respectant la pluralité des expériences vécues, le cinéma peut contribuer à la construction d'une mémoire collective intégrant les différentes réalités d'un pays où l'histoire varie souvent d'une communauté à l'autre. Parallèlement, cet axe étudie les défis méthodologiques liés à la rédaction d'une histoire du cinéma libanais. La production cinématographique nationale se caractérise par son aspect fragmenté et éparpillé, ce qui rend complexe la constitution d'une chronologie unifiée. Bien que des chercheurs et chercheuses tels que Mohamed Soueid, Ibrahim al-Ariss, Hady Zaccak, Lina Khatib, Elie Yazbek ou Raphaël Millet, aient publié des ouvrages de référence pour structurer cette mémoire, l'éparpillage des films et des sources primaires demeure un obstacle majeur. Il s'agira ici de rassembler ces fragments de patrimoine pour établir une historiographie du cinéma libanais. L'objectif est de comprendre comment ces deux formes d'écritures s'influencent mutuellement pour définir l'identité culturelle et historique du Liban. Pour guider les communications, cet axe propose quelques pistes de réflexion non exhaustives : · La représentation cinématographique de l'histoire du Liban · L'historiographie du cinéma libanais : défis et méthodes · Cinéma libanais et mémoire collective III) Pratiques artistiques contemporaines Les pratiques contemporaines de l'image nous invitent à penser le cinéma libanais actuel dans d'autres espaces que celui de la salle de projection traditionnelle : lieu d'exposition, réseaux sociaux, plateformes en ligne sont autant de zones - réelles ou virtuelles - au sein desquelles le cinéma libanais se transforme. Des installations multimédia de Lamia Joreige aux films-performances de Rabih Mroué et Lina Saneh, l'objet-film dépasse le cadre classique de la narration pour devenir un lieu d'expérimentation et de recherches. Irriguées par des pratiques d'hybridation, de « trafiquage », de fragmentation, les démarches des cinéastes libanais contemporains (tels que Sirine Fattouh, Ghassan Salhab, Akram Zaatari, Joana Hadjithomas et Khalil Joreige, etc) nous invitent à repenser notre rapport aux images, au réel et à la fiction. Les communications pourront proposer tous types d'approches méthodologiques (analyse filmique, approche génétique, recherche-création, études culturelles). Pour orienter les futur•es intervenant•es, nous proposons quelques pistes de réflexion non exhaustives : · Hybridation des formes : l'image comme terrain d'expérimentation · Les représentations filmiques contemporaines face aux représentations médiatiques · Politiques des images, éthiques du regard et nouveaux récits IV) Les pratiques archivistiques autour du cinéma libanais Des documents de travail en amont du tournage à ceux qui accompagnent la diffusion des œuvres, les archives non-film du cinéma libanais constituent des ressources majeures pour la recherche en études cinématographiques. Appliquées au cas libanais, elles offrent la possibilité de retracer la circulation des films tout en éclairant les conditions matérielles, institutionnelles et discursives de leur production et de leur réception à différentes époques. À partir de la critique de presse, des affiches, des photographies de plateau et de promotion, des carnets de notes, des scénarios annotés, de la correspondance professionnelle ou de tout autre document afférent à la fabrique et à la circulation des œuvres, cet axe invite à interroger les archives non-film du cinéma libanais afin d'en dégager les régimes de réception successifs, ainsi que les modalités d'accès, différenciées selon les aires géographiques et les périodes historiques, tant aux films eux-mêmes qu'aux savoirs qui s'élaborent à leur sujet. Parallèlement, la préservation de ces fonds mobilise au Liban un ensemble de structures engagées dans un travail soutenu de collecte, d'indexation, de restauration, de numérisation et de valorisation. L'Association Jocelyne Saab, UMAM Documentation and Research, Nadi lekol el Nas, Cinémathèque Beyrouth, l'Arab Image Foundation (AIF), la Bibliothèque Orientale, les Non-Aligned Film Archives, ainsi que plusieurs collections privées (Abboudi Abou Jaoudé, Hady Zaccak, etc.) constituent autant d'instances à partir desquelles se reformulent aujourd'hui les enjeux de conservation, de restitution et de transmission des archives. Les communications pourront mobiliser une pluralité d'approches méthodologiques (analyse filmique, approche génétique, recherche-création, inventaire et valorisation de corpus au sein des organisations, études culturelles). Afin d'orienter les futur·es intervenant·es, nous proposons quelques pistes de réflexion, à titre non exhaustif : · La restitution des archives non-films et l'enjeu de leur circulation · Pratiques Archivistiques : Collecte, Préservation, conservation, restauration et valorisation des archives · Politiques des archives : le patrimoine libanais, les institutions nationales et les structures culturelles indépendantes Les propositions de communication, d'une longueur de 300 à 500 mots, accompagnées d'une courte biographie de l'auteur (150 mots), sont à envoyer avant le 30 septembre 2026 à colloquecinemalibanais@gmail.com Les communications ne devront pas dépasser les 20 minutes. Une réponse sera communiquée avant le 31 octobre 2026. Les frais de déplacement seront à la charge des participants. Comité d'organisation : Evelyne Hlais Université Saint-Joseph de Beyrouth, Université de Caen - Normandie, Michael Issa El Helou Université Saint-Joseph de Beyrouth, Université de Caen - Normandie, Rosalie Tenaillon Chackal Université Saint-Joseph de Beyrouth, Université de Poitiers, Hady Zaccak Université Saint-Joseph de Beyrouth, Université Paris 1 Panthéon-Sorbonne. Comité scientifique : Robert Bonamy Professeur des universités en études cinématographiques (Université de Poitiers) Fabien Boully Maître de conférences en études cinématographiques (Université Paris Nanterre) Mathilde Rouxel Docteure en études cinématographiques Ghada Sayegh Professeure associée en études cinématographiques (Université Saint-Joseph de Beyrouth) Michel Tabet Anthropologue, Chargé de recherche CNRS - Centre national de la recherche scientifique Laurent Véray Professeur d'études cinématographiques et audiovisuelles (Université Sorbonne Nouvelle - Paris 3) Valérie Vignaux Professeure en études cinématographiques (Université de Caen-Normandie) Elie Yazbek Professeur des universités en études cinématographiques (Université Saint-Joseph de Beyrouth) Bibliographie indicative : BEDJAOUI, Ahmed et SERCEAU, Michel (dir.). items.title: Cinema, Philosophy, and Ethical Criticism
items.excerpt: Contemporary philosophy and modern aesthetics have long stressed the potential of cinema to engage itself in the act of thinking and philosophical reflections through the presentation and assembly of moving images and sound. This presumed capacity to think and philosophize is realized by its spectators, who engage with the visual, auditory, and textual el...
items.fullDescription: Contemporary philosophy and modern aesthetics have long stressed the potential of cinema to engage itself in the act of thinking and philosophical reflections through the presentation and assembly of moving images and sound. This presumed capacity to think and philosophize is realized by its spectators, who engage with the visual, auditory, and textual elements interwoven in films, resulting in a fusion that sparks novel thoughts and philosophical insights. However, philosophers such as Gilles Deleuze and Stanley Cavell, extend this idea further by arguing that cinema not only has the ability to engage in philosophical debates but also, in so doing, brings about a series of moral consequences and implications that warrant further investigation into its potential to stimulate ethical thinking. Therefore, this panel invites scholars from diverse disciplines to explore the intersection of cinema, philosophy, and ethics. We will address the central questions: Can cinema genuinely engage in philosophical thinking? What are the ethical implications of this approach to cinema? In what ways can cinema engage in philosophy and ethics? Themes of interest include, but are not limited to, the following: · Cinema, Philosophy, and Ethics · Cinema and Ethical Criticism · Phenomenology, Ethics, and World Cinema · Gilles Deleuze and Time-Image · Stanley Cavell and Moral Perfectionism · Emmanuel Levinas and the Ethics of the Face-to-Face Encounter

items.title: CFP-NeMLA: When Lives Do Things: Lives as Archives
items.location: Newport, Rhode Island · Northeast Modern Language Association
items.excerpt: As Sidonie Smith and Julia Watson argue, "autobiographical storytelling is a performative act through which narrators construct rather than simply describe selves." In recent times, "life writing" has become a familiar term in literary and cultural studies, encompassing a range of written texts, including autobiographies, biographies, letters, memoirs, sl...
items.fullDescription: As Sidonie Smith and Julia Watson argue, "autobiographical storytelling is a performative act through which narrators construct rather than simply describe selves." In recent times, "life writing" has become a familiar term in literary and cultural studies, encompassing a range of written texts, including autobiographies, biographies, letters, memoirs, slave narratives, prison narratives, captivity narratives, travel narratives, confessionals, conversion narratives and even in recent times, personal diaries. However, rather than remain transparent records of lived experience, life writings actively shape identities, produce historical knowledge, preserve cultural memory, and intervene in social and political worlds. This panel invites scholars to reconsider what life writing does beyond just what it says. We invite contributions that challenge conventional approaches to reading life writings. We are particularly interested in scholarships that examine how life writings preserve marginalized histories, challenge dominant archives, and create alternative ways of knowing. We also welcome papers that investigate life writing across historical periods, geographical locations, and disciplinary approaches. Possible topics include, but are not limited to: Life writing as archive. Counter-archive or alternative historical record. Testimony, witnessing, and the ethics of narration. Life writing and the production of race, gender, sexuality, or class. Spiritual, religious, and ritual dimensions of autobiographical expression. Collective, communal, and relational forms of selfhood. Life writing and memory studies. Digital, visual, and multimodal life narratives. Abstracts of 250-300 words and a short bio (100 words) should be submitted via: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22538 Session modality:Hybrid (in-person but accepting remote presentations).

items.title: Talk it through: Writing Pedagogy and Talking in an LLM World
items.excerpt: Maybe no one has ever looked at a pile of papers with unbridled joy, but in a time of university-subsidized LLMs, instructors are more likely view a stack of papers with a mix of dread and horror. items.fullDescription: Maybe no one has ever looked at a pile of papers with unbridled joy, but in a time of university-subsidized LLMs, instructors are more likely view a stack of papers with a mix of dread and horror. How many made up quotations will I have to check? How many fake citations? How many versions of "it's not ___, it's ___" can I read before my soul leaves my body? To teach writing now is to be reminded too forcefully of the critique of writing that Plato relays in the Phaedrus: "it is no true wisdom that you offer your disciples, but only its semblance, for by telling them of many things without teaching them you will make them seem to know much, while for the most part they know nothing, and as men filled, not with wisdom but with the conceit of wisdom, they will be a burden to their fellows." Plato captures here complaints that writing teachers pestered by LLM-extruded prose should recognize immediately. Many writing teachers have responded to the creation of the semblance of wisdom and its corrosive production of conceit by turning to the solution offered in Plato: the voice. This roundtable invites participants who have shifted their pedagogy to center discussion, dialogue, oral presentation, and other forms of embodied communication in response to the depredations of LLMs in higher education. How has this use of voice affected how students write and how we think about their writing? Submit abstracts of 250 words by September 30, 2026. For inquiries, contact Joshua Gooch (goochj@dyc.edu) or Sara Sullivan (ssulliva@sbu.edu).

items.fullDescription: CFP for NeMLA 2027 Conference Virtual Only Panel Oral Community Knowledge as Antidote for Empire in Anglophone Literature If Empire thrives and even conquers through written text: codification of laws and bureaucracy, cartography of territory, travelogues of the exotic Other, it equally seeks to suppress oral indigenous knowledge of the vanquished. Yet orality can also be the site of resistance and community identity for just such a reason. This panel seeks proposals of works that investigate how orality of form and/or content in Anglophone literature disrupts Empire and empowers community. The irony of pursuing orality in literature, which is historically written, is not lost on us, but also provides an opportunity for creative, resilient exploration. Some possible ways of approaching this topic may include: Accent and dialect Pidgins, creoles, and contact languages Translations and translators Language and identity Oral folk wisdom and stories Glossaries and indices Textual hybridity or multimodality Literacy This is a Call for Papers for the NeMLA 2027 Conference. Submissions will only be accepted through the NeMLA portal, with a deadline of September 30, 2026 . This panel is Virtual Only , regardless of whether the participant is physically present at the conference. Paper proposals can be submitted here: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22648. Only submissions submitted through the NeMLA portal will be considered. For questions, please feel free to email Dr. Dorisa Costello at dcostello@jessup.edu.

Specifically, we wish to commemorate the centenary of the publication of Heidegger's "Sein und Zeit"/Being and...
items.fullDescription: This panel, commemorating the centenary of the 1927 publication of "Being and Time," has a general and specific focus. Generally, we will explore the relationship between literature and philosophy without historical and geographical limitations. Specifically, we wish to commemorate the centenary of the publication of Heidegger's "Sein und Zeit"/Being and Time in 1927 as it relates to the practice and theory of literature. Our "specific" avenue of exploration involves at least four possible thematic contexts: · Literary influences on Heidegger. The existential analyses in "Being and Time" reflect many well-known and researched themes such as anxiety, death, dread, fear, curiosity, alienation, the crowd, boredom, responsibility, the experience of time and many others. One might explore the cultural and literary origins of these terms that in "BT" are seen in an ontological perspective. · Literary influences of Heidegger. Many novelists and poets have been influenced by Heidegger. Examples might be Günther Grass, Sartre, Alain Robbe-Grillet, Claude Simon, Iris Murdoch, Walter Percy, Milan Kundera, Jon Fosse, J.M. Coetzee and many others. · Thematic Connections between "Being and Time" and novelists and poets. Virtually all literary modernism and post-modernism explore the theme of temporality and presentations on these connections would be valued. · The influence of "Being and Time" on literary theory after 1927. The literary reflection of Derrida, Deleuze, Foucault, Lacan, Sartre and many others has been influenced by Heidegger's "Being and Time." While the language of this session will be English, we welcome the use of the German text as well as primary texts in languages other than English. These texts will be circulated before the March 2027 conference.

items.fullDescription: Conference: 2-4 December 2026, in Gdansk, Poland and online CALL FOR PAPERS: In our modern world, which some have argued to be disjointed while immersing itself ever deeper in crisis, the turning back to "the olden days" and the ensuing nostalgia constitute anoticeable phenomenon, both individually (the memory of biography) and collectively (the memory of history). Another important - and equally noticeable - phenomenon is the longing for something vague, indefinite or never existent. Hence, during our interdisciplinary conference we would like to concentrate on the phenomena of nostalgia and melancholy. We are interested in all expressions of longing forthe past, from the reassuring and action-propelling ones to those which paralyze us, leading to despair and utter dejection. We aim to explore the experience of nostalgia and melancholy in its multifarious manifestations: psychological, social, historical, cultural, philosophical, religious, economic, political, artistic, and many others. We will also devote considerable attention to how these phenomena appear and transform in artistic practices: literature, film, theatre, and visual arts. This is why we invite researchers representing various academic disciplines: anthropology, history, psychology, psychoanalysis, sociology, politics, philosophy, economics, law, literary studies, theatre studies, film studies, memory studies, nostalgia studies, migration studies, consciousness studies, dream studies, gender studies, postcolonial studies, medical sciences, psychiatry, cognitive sciences, design, project management and other disciplines. Different forms of presentations are encouraged, including case studies, theoretical investigations, problem-oriented arguments, and comparative analyses. We will be happy to hear from both experienced scholars and young academics at the beginning of their careers, as well as students. Rocks, minerals, fossils, sediments, ruins, and other more-than-living material formations are no longer understood merely as settings or symbols, but as archi...
items.fullDescription: Across contemporary literary and cultural studies, increasing attention has been devoted to the ways in which geological matter shapes cultural imagination, environmental memory, and literary form. Rocks, minerals, fossils, sediments, ruins, and other more-than-living material formations are no longer understood merely as settings or symbols, but as archives of deep time, environmental change, historical violence, and planetary entanglement. This panel invites papers exploring how literature engages with the Earth as a site of memory, relation, and meaning-making. We are particularly interested in approaches that examine geological formations, landscapes, material traces, planetary archives, and sedimented histories as participants in literary and cultural processes rather than passive backdrops to human experience. Possible topics include geologies of memory; deep time and literary temporality; environmental trauma and material archives; fossils, ruins, and traces; sacred landscapes and earth-based imaginaries; extraction, waste, and environmental justice; posthumanist and new materialist approaches to literature; relational poetics; and the intersections of geology, ecology, and cultural memory. We welcome contributions addressing literary and cultural texts from any historical period, linguistic tradition, or geographical region, as well as comparative and transnational perspectives. The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. Please clearly indicate in your submission whether you wish to participate in person or remotely. All abstract submissions must be submitted through the NeMLA portal https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22630. Please note that the submission window for individual abstracts officially opens June 15 and closes September 30.

items.title: Horror in Tragedy / Tragedy in Horror
items.excerpt: While tragedy and horror are often treated as distinct genres, both traditions ask audiences to look steadily at what ordinarily repels them. From unburied corpses and undead monsters to violated kinship and failed agency, these spectacles produce fear, pity, disgust, and sorrow alongside fascination. This session explores what might be understood as a tr...
items.fullDescription: While tragedy and horror are often treated as distinct genres, both traditions ask audiences to look steadily at what ordinarily repels them. From unburied corpses and undead monsters to violated kinship and failed agency, these spectacles produce fear, pity, disgust, and sorrow alongside fascination. This session explores what might be understood as a tragedy-horror continuum, examining the aesthetic, affective, and ritual overlaps between tragic and horrific representation across historical periods and media. This panel welcomes interdisciplinary approaches to the relationship between tragedy and horror in theater, film, literature, television, performance, and visual culture. Possible topics include abjection, affect, taboo, pollution, spectatorship, ritual, violence, mourning, trauma, revenge, monstrosity, catharsis, embodiment, psychoanalysis, genre theory, and adaptation. Papers may address how tragedy and horror construct communal affective experiences; how art and literature shape cultural understandings of taboo and trauma; or how horror inherits, transforms, or reimagines tragic structures. The significance of this session lies in its effort to create a space for dialogue between fields that are often separated institutionally and generically despite their shared concerns with suffering, transgression, and collective spectatorship. By bringing tragedy and horror studies into conversation, this panel seeks to reconsider the boundaries between canonical and popular forms while illuminating how these shared spectacles mediate communal encounters with what culture cannot easily assimilate. ___ Please submit abstracts by September 30, 2026 through the NeMLA portal: https://cfplist.com/nemla/User/SubmitAbstract/22290

items.fullDescription: This panel presents a review of the musical, artistic, literary, social, political, and cultural expressions of Bad Bunny, Benito Antonio Martinez Ocasio. The panel centers on aspects of African diasporic experiences, i.e., Afro-Latinidad. The panel considers Blackness as gleaned and embedded from Bad Bunny's musical repertoire and uses Aurora Santiago Ortiz and Jorell Melendez-Badillo's framework of "historical memory" and "limits of nostalgia" as presented in their piece "(Nada es) 'Como antes.'" The panel seeks to utilize this framework and focus on Black presence in all its intersecting and non-intersecting forms involving race, class, gender, and sex. Specifically, papers are sought that examine strategies and uses of, for example, an "idyllic past" and/or "Great Mixed Family" motif and show ruptures in representations that subsume or otherize Blackness. Submissions can interpret outside of Bad Bunny and include other artists venturing into its use in other realms and/or aspects of appreciation or appropriation discourse, read broadly and sublimated in text. The panel discusses the uses of various musical, visual, aural, and literary works to deconstruct language use, song beats, films, concerts, and media, similar to Bad Bunny's use of House, Hip Hop, Trap, Drill, Reggaeton, Bachata, Afro Punk, etc. A key element is locating, gleaning, or demystifying aspects of Blackness, construed broadly, in Bad Bunny's context or in other works that offer perspectives on Black embeddedness and involve authenticity, appreciation, and the intersection of race, class, gender, or sex. Papers are sought that review the application, infusion, or parallelism of the Black past infusing Bomba, Plena, Son, Salsa, Trap, Drill, Reggaeton, and other genres, as merged running threads. This panel invites papers that explore the musical, artistic, literary, social, political, and cultural expressions of Bad Bunny (Benito Antonio Martinez Ocasio). We focus on African diasporic, or AfroLatinidad, experiences. The panel examines how Blackness construed in various forms appears in Bad Bunny's music and/or is subsumed within it. The panel delves into the intersections of race, class, gender, and sex, and the recognition of the African presence, identity, or representation.

items.title: Apocalypse or Utopia: Whose End of the World?
items.excerpt: Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. Primary Areas of interest: Global Anglophone / Cultural Studies and Media Studies Abstract: Since at least the middle of the 20th century, we have seen few, if any, utopian texts speculating about positive potential worlds. Instead, we have seen a deluge of dystopia...
items.fullDescription: Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. Primary Areas of interest: Global Anglophone / Cultural Studies and Media Studies Abstract: Since at least the middle of the 20th century, we have seen few, if any, utopian texts speculating about positive potential worlds. Instead, we have seen a deluge of dystopian, apocalyptic, and post-apocalyptic texts foretelling the end of the world. This begs the question, why have utopian texts become such a scarce literary genre? What is it about our time that makes speculative literature turn away from the utopic and instead toward its more nihilistic counterparts? Our societal fascination with apocalyptic literature has not gone unnoticed. Writing in 1979, H. Bruce Franklin identifies this cultural anxiety in his essay, "What Are We to Make of J. G. Ballard's Apocalypse," where he claims that the post-World War II decolonial liberation movements and the collapse of the European empires were the source of this apocalyptic obsession. On the other hand, the utopian genre is often acknowledged as beginning with Thomas More's Utopia (1516), written within the burgeoning rise of capitalism and the colonial expansion of early 16th century Europe. Hythloday, More's fictional narrator, finds Utopia within the New World, where the undiscovered territories and peoples outside of Europe became the catalysts that made the early utopian imagination possible. That the European utopian genre came at the expense of the existing worlds of indigenous peoples might also prompt us to think about how apocalypse factors into the utopian imagination. Since Franklin's essay, this fixation with the end of the world has only intensified, driven to new heights by the overwhelming evidence of climate change's devastation, global pandemics, and catastrophic geopolitical conflicts. Franklin concludes that Ballard's novels are unable to make a distinction between the end of capitalism and the end of the world itself. This prompts us ask: whose "world" is ending and how might it make way for a new one?

We are interested in discussing the incorporation of any and all forms of games and gaming, including, but not limited to: - board, tabletop, and card games - social deduction games - tabletop role-playing systems such as Dungeons and Dragons - invented role-playing scenarios - gamification of in-class activities - video and digital games Abstract / Overview Tabletop gaming and literary studies share a core essence: storytelling. As we aim to teach students in our writing, rhetoric, composition, and literature courses the values and techniques of narrative, we might choose to borrow from the myriad strategies developed in the world of board and role-playing games in order to engage learners on an even more visceral level. Applying approaches of play and pretend, whether through published or in-class created gaming scenarios, educators can enable rich modes of experiential learning. Such learning has the potential to bond our classroom communities more profoundly than verbal chatter alone may accomplish-and, perhaps, to undergird our intrinsic human need to build shared, lived encounters in ways that machines and AI cannot simulate. items.title: Resisting from the Edge: Marginal Characters and Collective Empowerment
items.location: Salve Regina University, Newport, RI · NeMLA
items.excerpt: This panel invites papers that examine the agency of marginalized characters in literary, visual, audiovisual, and artistic productions from the French?speaking world across any historical period. Artistic works offer numerous examples of figures who maintain an ambivalent relationship to their communities. Because of their social or ethnic origins, their...
items.fullDescription: This panel invites papers that examine the agency of marginalized characters in literary, visual, audiovisual, and artistic productions from the French?speaking world across any historical period. Artistic works offer numerous examples of figures who maintain an ambivalent relationship to their communities. Because of their social or ethnic origins, their divergent perspectives, or their transgression of established norms, certain characters find themselves pushed to the margins of social, familial, or professional groups. While such exclusion may appear to weaken those who experience it, this panel asks whether marginalization might also function as a source of strength. Can marginality become a generative position? Do marginalized individuals succeed in forming new communities that enable them to confront stigma and ultimately overcome it? This discussion seeks to shift our understanding of the place occupied by marginalized individuals and groups in fictional narratives. By considering the networks of solidarity that emerge among marginalized subjects, the panel explores what lies beneath their presumed inferiority, inviting critical attention to what is hidden, erased, or rendered invisible within dominant narratives. More importantly, we will examine how marginalized communities may become empowering spaces of resistance, mutual support, and hope. Drawing on approaches from cultural studies, postcolonial theory, sociolinguistics, and intersectionality, this session seeks to understand how literary, visual, audiovisual, and artistic narratives stage the tensions between marginality and belonging, strength and vulnerability. Proposals engaging with environmental humanities, posthumanism, Indigenous studies, as well as diaspora and migration studies are strongly encouraged. We also welcome submissions drawing on gender and queer studies, memory and trauma studies, digital humanities, or other interdisciplinary frameworks within the humanities. We welcome proposals of 250-300 words, in French or English, submitted by September 30.

items.title: Beyond Hegemony: Masculinities in World and Comparative Literature
items.excerpt: This panel examines how literary and cultural texts from diverse global traditions imagine masculinity not merely as an individual identity but as a relational and communal practice. In an era marked by migration, political polarization, social fragmentation, environmental crisis, and renewed struggles for collective empowerment, narratives of masculinity...
items.fullDescription: This panel examines how literary and cultural texts from diverse global traditions imagine masculinity not merely as an individual identity but as a relational and communal practice. In an era marked by migration, political polarization, social fragmentation, environmental crisis, and renewed struggles for collective empowerment, narratives of masculinity have become central sites through which communities negotiate belonging, responsibility, vulnerability, and resistance. While dominant models of masculinity have often been associated with hierarchy, authority, nationalism, and exclusion, contemporary world literatures increasingly depict alternative masculinities grounded in care, ethical responsibility, emotional openness, solidarity, and collective survival. Such representations challenge inherited structures of power while opening new possibilities for community formation and social transformation. This panel invites comparative and transnational approaches that explore how literary texts, films, cultural narratives, and other expressive forms construct, critique, or reimagine masculinities within local and global communities. Particular attention may be given to how gender intersects with race, class, migration, coloniality, religion, ecology, technology, and cultural memory in shaping collective identities and practices of empowerment. Situated within the broader concerns of World Literature and Comparative Literature, this session seeks to foster dialogue across regions, languages, and traditions while investigating how masculinities participate in the creation-or disruption-of empowered communities. We welcome papers addressing, but not limited to: Comparative constructions of masculinity across world literatures Masculinity and migration, diaspora, or displacement Literary representations of fatherhood, mentorship, and communal responsibility Masculinities in Global South literatures Masculinity and environmental or ecological communities Masculinity, memory, and post-conflict reconstruction Queer and non-hegemonic masculinities in world literature Masculinity and cultural resistance under authoritarian regimes Masculinity, religion, and collective belonging Masculinity and affect in contemporary global fiction

items.fullDescription: Our session examines the importance of women as fictional characters, authors, and purveyors of legacies associated with the Great War of 1914-1918. It is hoped that we can bring a fresh critical light to this pivotal moment in world history. Too often, scholarly reflection on the Great War tends to focus military activity and various aspects of masculine performance. We would like to use a wider lens for our discussion of the Great War. Please note the very wide range of perspectives in this seminar: authors, characters, and context. More precisely, our reconsideration of the works, authors, and legacies of the Great War includes three directions: first, the examination of women appearing as fictional characters in this normally male-oriented thematic area; second, reflection on women writers, artists, and filmmakers engaged in representing the Great War; finally, establishing historical continuities deriving from the relationship between women and the Great War. The flexible methodology of this session includes the examination of novels, poems, paintings, photography, film, documentaries, memoirs, and memorials. A few suggestions for interpretative directions: · Recent critical directions on topics relating to women and the Great War · Non-European, global writing on the Great War · Themes of colonialism, migration, class, public health, punition, sexual violence, and race · The inclusion of documentary, archival, and any relevant internet materials · Presentations on "Women, New England, and the Great War" · The involvement of women in medical and public health issues during the Great War · Women in the Great War as represented in museums, local, national, and global media.

items.title: "Why Am I Reading This?" Finding Literary Relevance
items.excerpt: If you've taught older literature recently, you've probably heard students ask the question "Why am I reading this?" They've got a point. Why should we read Shakespeare's plays, or Paradise Lost , or (god-forbid) "The Rape of the Lock"? We might be able to coax students into reading a Jane Austen novel or even Huckleberry Finn because they are still part...
items.fullDescription: If you've taught older literature recently, you've probably heard students ask the question "Why am I reading this?" They've got a point. Why should we read Shakespeare's plays, or Paradise Lost , or (god-forbid) "The Rape of the Lock"? We might be able to coax students into reading a Jane Austen novel or even Huckleberry Finn because they are still part of our cultural imagination, but it's less easy to coax students into reading Beowulf or Tom Jones -texts that are less familiar in form and content. As we jettison the canon and the timeless truths of literature, what do we expect reading older texts will give our students that they wouldn't otherwise understand? How do we explain our reasoning to students about why they should read texts from earlier literary periods? How do we present these earlier periods (or periodization) and their culture and ideas as relevant to our undergraduate students in our English, writing, or humanities programs? Or is relevance the problem? How might we redefine older literature in our classes, or what we (can) do with these texts? I'm particularly interested in position papers that take into consideration practical pedagogical and theoretical concerns: arguments about curricula, course development or assignments that do different things with this literature. Papers are welcome on any kind of literature/texts or periods prior to 1800.

items.title: Collective Selves: Rethinking Autobiography Beyond the Individual.
items.location: Salve Regina University, New Port Rhode Island · NeMLA
items.excerpt: Collective Selves: Rethinking Autobiography Beyond the Individual. Philippe Lejeune's influential formulation of the autobiographical pact has long shaped autobiography studies. The pact presumes an aligned author, narrator, and protagonist, and simultaneously privileges the singular, first-person life narrative as the normative autobiographical form. How...
items.fullDescription: Collective Selves: Rethinking Autobiography Beyond the Individual. Philippe Lejeune's influential formulation of the autobiographical pact has long shaped autobiography studies. The pact presumes an aligned author, narrator, and protagonist, and simultaneously privileges the singular, first-person life narrative as the normative autobiographical form. However, several personal narratives resist these assumptions. Edited autobiographies, oral histories, collaborative memoirs, and interview-based narratives often construct an autobiographical "I"/protagonist that is multiple rather than singular and that emerges across the voices of individuals whose experiences collectively narrate a shared history. Likewise, some collective life narratives depart from conventional autobiography structures, appearing as fragmented accounts, interwoven testimonies, or dispersed chapters within larger texts. This panel invites papers that examine forms of self-representation that foreground the collective. Particularly of interest are papers that interrogate the presumed unity of author, narrator, and protagonist; reconsider the autobiographical pact in light of collaborative and collective forms of narration; and explore how communities write themselves into history through shared acts of remembrance, testimony, and self-representation. Topics to consider include, but are not limited to: Collaborative and edited autobiographies Oral histories and interview-based life narratives Community and collective testimony Indigenous, Black, and diasporic collective life writing Gender, Sexuality and self testaments Archives, memory, and community self-representation Theoretical reconsiderations of the autobiographical pact and authorial voice Experimental and hybrid forms of autobiography Collective authorship, voice, and narrative authority Life writing, activism, and community formation. By centering collective modes of self-account, this panel seeks to expand the boundaries of autobiography studies and establish interventions that consider how communities, rather than individuals alone, become autobiographical subjects.

items.fullDescription: Philip Kan Gotanda's play Yankee Dawg You Die centers a conflict between two Asian American actors. The aging Vincent Chang built his career playing bumbling Japanese soldiers and one-dimensional Asian stereotypes in classic Hollywood. He's confronted by the up-and-coming Bradley Yamashita, who insists that Asian (American) characters should be played with dignity and accuracy. The heart of the play is an ambivalence about Asian (American) representation and whether viewers should sympathize with Chang for taking the only acting jobs available or with Yamashita in his condemnation of the racist roles-or with both. This panel seeks to explore comparable ambivalences in Asian/diasporic representation. We specifically seek papers about contemporary attempts to re-negotiate or reclaim depictions of Asian people, culture, literature, myth, history, etc. The papers may examine any form of media and utilize any theoretical lens, as long as the media in question seek to address anti-Asian racism or stereotypes, or to explore ambiguities in Asian/diasporic experience. "Asian" for the purposes of this panel, includes anyone (descended) from the Asian continent. By challenging imagery and cultural conceptions that stereotype or limit Asian people, this panel intends to engage the conference theme by exploring empowerments of Asian communities, whether in Asia or abroad. Please submit a 200-300 word abstract at https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22330 by 30 Sept.

Modalities in literature can be narrative, descriptive, persuasive, expository, causal, effectual, definitive, comparative, and argumentative. Through close and careful examination of "voice," "word," or "non-verbal expressions" of speaker or writer, someone might discover surprising probabilities, poignant certainties, unexpected opportunities, awakened obligations, absurd necessities, or unusual permissions that shift from "a state of passivity to being active agents of change." This roundtable session will explore, define, determine, and discuss how key modalities in various literatures have the ability to excite, intrigue, and empower communities--large and small, academic and personal, collective and individual-to values of positivity and collectiveness.

items.fullDescription: Call for Papers NeMLA's 58th Annual Convention - "Empowering Communities" March 6-9, 2027 | Salve Regina University, Newport, Rhode Island (Hybrid Conference) Panel Title: Tracing Female Body, Art & Economy in the Global South Primary Area / Secondary Area: Women's and Gender Studies / Interdisciplinary Humanities Modality: Hybrid Chair: Maryam Raza (University of Auckland, New Zealand) This panel explores how the historical treatment of female bodies in the Global South through the patriarchal structures of subjectification, commodification, sexualization and objectification has been reversed by contemporary strategies of resistance and reclamation. The panel examines how the postmodern woman in diverse socio-economic contexts "trains" the masculine gaze (Dixit 2024) over her body, renegotiating agency. Women increasingly reconfigure the female body through carnivalesque, alternative spaces of identity, creativity and skill through embodied forms. The female body evolves from a passive object of desire into an active, expressive, and economically productive site. Thus, women transform spaces of suppression to places of resistance and empowerment. Hussain et al. (2025) note that emancipation precedes autonomy and decision-making in socio-economic spheres. The panel further examines the intersection of bodily narratives with performative femininity, wellness cultures, fitness regimes, and beauty and anti-aging industries. It also restructures the domestic space as a site of culinary entrepreneurship, aided by digital platforms. Consequently, the Global South woman utilizes such arenas to enhance visibility, economic upward mobility, and global networks beyond what Fahmida Riaz termed the "Four Walled Homestead." Drawing on interdisciplinary approaches from cultural, gender, media, literary, and postcolonial studies, this panel foregrounds how women collectively construct bodily identities that both challenge and adapt to tradition. The selected papers will revolve around the following themes: Women and wellness culture Gender, food, and culinary skills Ageing Dance Cultures Women and Performing Arts Fetishizing and infantilizing women within beauty industry Submission Deadline : September 30, 2026 Please submit abstracts via the NeMLA portal: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22411 For queries about the panel, prospective participants are warmly encouraged to contact the chair: Maryam Raza mraz655@aucklanduni.ac.nz

items.title: Literary and Cultural Communities Between Asia and the Hispanophone World
items.excerpt: From the early European missionaries who ventured into Asia all the way to the contemporary networks of connection that currently link Spain and Latin America countries like China, Japan and Korea, the cultural, literary and artistic links between Asia and the Hispanophone world run deep. Based on religious, linguistic, cultural, literary or artistic poin...
items.fullDescription: From the early European missionaries who ventured into Asia all the way to the contemporary networks of connection that currently link Spain and Latin America countries like China, Japan and Korea, the cultural, literary and artistic links between Asia and the Hispanophone world run deep. Based on religious, linguistic, cultural, literary or artistic points of contact, numerous groups and communities have maintained active and productive lines of connection that have transform their own localities through the implementation of imported ideas, products, concepts from the other side of the world. Considering the long-standing lines of dialogue between these two vast geographic spaces, we invite scholars reflecting on questions such as: How Hispanophone writers and artists have searched in Asian literary practices spaces for renewal and innovation? How communities from Asian countries have assimilated and reinterpreted Latin American cultural products? How do the cultural products and creative practices of the two sides of the Pacific Ocean mirror/distort/reinvent each other? Papers dealing with literature, theatre, cinema, and other cultural productions are welcome. This is board-sponsored panel accepts presentations in English and Spanish are welcome.

items.title: Bodies, Borders, and Belonging: Comparative Perspectives on South Korean and Italian Women Writers
items.excerpt: This panel explores the intersections between contemporary South Korean and Italian women's writing through the lenses of memory, gender, trauma, migration, and post-national identity. While these literary traditions are often studied separately within distinct geopolitical and linguistic frameworks, the panel proposes a comparative and transnational appr...
items.fullDescription: This panel explores the intersections between contemporary South Korean and Italian women's writing through the lenses of memory, gender, trauma, migration, and post-national identity. While these literary traditions are often studied separately within distinct geopolitical and linguistic frameworks, the panel proposes a comparative and transnational approach that foregrounds shared concerns surrounding patriarchal structures, historical violence, intergenerational memory, and the negotiation of belonging. Bringing together scholars working on Italian and Korean women writers, the panel examines how literature becomes a space for articulating marginalized experiences and challenging dominant national narratives. The selected texts engage with themes such as family silences, colonial and postwar legacies, diaspora, racialization, feminist activism, labor, and the body as an archive of memory. Through memoir, autofiction, and experimental narrative forms, these writers construct alternative genealogies and forms of counter-memory that complicate homogeneous understandings of identity and nationhood. The panel seeks to foster an interdisciplinary conversation between Asian Studies, Italian Studies, Comparative Literature, Gender Studies, and Migration Studies. By placing South Korean and Italian women writers in dialogue, the session highlights the productive possibilities of comparative feminist methodologies across different cultural contexts while emphasizing shared strategies of resistance, care, and narrative reconstruction.

items.title: Paris Noir: Changes in the Margin
items.excerpt: This panel seeks papers on artistic movements, individuals or events that demonstrate how living as an outsider or in exile can facilitate cross-cultural learning and creating. The call is based on ideas of reterritorializing lost, outside, and minoritarian cultures such as the Négritude movement-which was recently celebrated at the center of the Paris-No...
items.fullDescription: This panel seeks papers on artistic movements, individuals or events that demonstrate how living as an outsider or in exile can facilitate cross-cultural learning and creating. The call is based on ideas of reterritorializing lost, outside, and minoritarian cultures such as the Négritude movement-which was recently celebrated at the center of the Paris-Noir exhibit at the Pompidou- and what Gertrude Stein calls the Lost Generation to find empowerment in exile. We seek to share techniques of research and teaching about the power of the powerless by studying overlooked artistic communities, individuals and cultures. For example we welcome submissions about how artists of the Paris Noir and/or Lost Generation relate to issues of culture, creating and community in our time. Many are unaware that James Baldwin, Miles Davis, and Jean-Michel Basquiat worked alongside Ernest Hemingway, Gertrude Stein and Henry Miller. These expatriates and African American artists electively inhabited the creative "Undercommons" of Paris Noir to remain generative despite fascism, racism, and war. These artists who were foreigners and outsiders are now classic and read inside the classroom by a majority in culture. Submit papers that analyze minorities, foreigners, strangers, exiles and outsiders in literature, film, or culture as a model for reflecting upon ways of reterritorializing the classroom and community. Below are some questions to orient your submission, although you are not limited to working on these: How does the marginalized turn the margin to a source of empowerment? What type of artist or work demonstrates change on the margins? What does it mean to inhabit to the role of foreigner, outsider, or minority? How does the existence of the stranger, immigrant, minority, or neighbor facilitate cross-cultural dialogue and generativity? How do we inhabit and engage vulnerability, process, child-like discovery, repetition and outside as a way of innovating that resists an authoritarian or artificial rule? Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. All abstract submissions must be submitted through the NeMLA portal. https://cfplist.com/nemla/ Home/S/22549 Chair: Dr. Jessica Datema, jdatema@bergen.edu, https://bergen.edu/academics/academic-divisions-departments/english-department/our-composition-literature-faculty/jessica-datema/ https://www.nemla.org/convention.html

Helmholtz Watson, a lecturer of Emotional Engineering, relates a story to Bernard Marx about being disciplined for sharing some of his poems with the students he was paid to indoctrinate. When asked what the poems had been about,...
items.fullDescription: In Aldous Huxley's Brave New World, humans are conditioned to seek out human companionship and to despise being by themselves. Helmholtz Watson, a lecturer of Emotional Engineering, relates a story to Bernard Marx about being disciplined for sharing some of his poems with the students he was paid to indoctrinate. When asked what the poems had been about, Helmholtz tells a surprised Bernard, "They were about being alone." Helmholtz, like many individuals in our world outside the novel, has a natural inclination to seek solitude. Some individuals choose to separate from community for short or long periods of time, retreating into the solitude of reflection and quiet. The flip side of solitude is loneliness, occurring when individuals experience unwanted distance from community and feel the lack of human connection. And then, there is an in-between state of those who simultaneously, and seemingly paradoxically, need copious alone time while also desiring community. This creative session will showcase readings of original works of poetry and prose by writers who compose about being alone, whether from a standpoint of loneliness, solitude, or somewhere in between. Poetry, fiction, and creative nonfiction are welcome in this session. Readers may also present a combination of genres. Proposals are accepted through September 30, 2026. Abstracts are 200-300 words in length, and titles should not exceed 100 characters. Please submit your abstract at the session's portal: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22377 Modality: This session is in hybrid format (in person, but will accept remote presentations). The 58th NeMLA convention will take place March 6-9, 2027, in Newport, RI. For more information on the convention and to view NeMLA's guidelines for abstracts, visit https://www.nemla.org Questions regarding the panel may be addressed to Lori Cummings at LoNewco1@wsc.edu

items.location: Salve Regina University (Newport, RI) · Northeast Modern Language Association

Session Abtract The guiding thread of this panel develops around a curious remark by Jacques Rancière that would seem to amend a classically Aristotelian thesis: "man is a...
items.fullDescription: Call for Papers -- NeMLA's 58th Annual Convention. Link: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22237 March 6-9, 2027. Newport, Rhode Island. Convention Site: Salve Regina University. Session Abtract The guiding thread of this panel develops around a curious remark by Jacques Rancière that would seem to amend a classically Aristotelian thesis: "man is a political animal because he is a literary animal," the fundamental condition of which is the "excess of words" constitutive of the human animal's politicalness. Literature (or poetry) has long been placed in close proximity to politics, as we find in Plato's Republic, where philosophy and literature-and, more generally, philosophy and politics-turn out to be perennial adversaries. That matters of literature bear upon the question of politics has featured in continental French thought for quite some time, considered in terms of the milieu of différance and event in Jacques Derrida; le partage du sensible and dissensus in Jacques Rancière; and the unpresentable and testimony to le différend in Jean-François Lyotard, among others. More recently, Geoffrey Bennington has taken up the question of the unreadable as that condition that keeps reading open to the event of alterity. What is at stake, then, in the complex relationship between literature and politics? How does literature bear witness to, or otherwise reconfigure, who counts as a speaking being, what is recognized as speech, and/or what falls out of discursive legibility as mere noise, excess, or silence? And how might mis-reading, mis-translation, and mis-understanding contribute to the politics of literature? This panel invites reflection on the figures, scenes, and events of textuality that respond to these and related questions. All critical approaches are welcome for this in-person panel. Eric J. Adamo McMaster University Hamilton, Ontario, Canada.

items.title: Reproductive (Un)silencing: Empowering Communities Through Creative Writing
items.location: Newport, Rhode Island or online · NeMLA
items.excerpt: In the definition given by Podnieks, matrifocal literatures are written and narrated by mothers in the first-person or limited third-person voice, rendering maternal identity and experience from subjective perspectives. Such narratives (re)value the significance and meaning of maternal figures in cultural, social, and national arenas, and often contest or...
items.fullDescription: In the definition given by Podnieks, matrifocal literatures are written and narrated by mothers in the first-person or limited third-person voice, rendering maternal identity and experience from subjective perspectives. Such narratives (re)value the significance and meaning of maternal figures in cultural, social, and national arenas, and often contest or negotiate traditional ideologies of the "good" mother as self-sacrificing, nurturing, and sexless with her antithesis, the "bad" mother. In the last few years, we have seen the rise of matrifocal writings published to break taboos about previously silenced reproductive experiences, create a sense of community, support, and care, and, overall, empower people. This creative session welcomes works of poetry, fiction, creative nonfiction, drama, essays, graphic novels, plays, and songs, among others - written from the point of view of the mother, by authors who identify as mothers, or engage in "maternal work" in the definition given by Ruddick - that address experiences of infertility, Assisted Reproductive Technology (ART), surrogacy, elective abortion, miscarriage, stillbirth, therapeutic abortion, fetal reduction, childbirth, breastfeeding, and postpartum, to mention a few. Mother-centered submissions related to topic such as contraception, sterilization, menstruation, menopause, and women's reproductive health in general are also encouraged.

items.title: Global and Intersectional Framings of Disability in Comics & Graphic Narratives
items.excerpt: Global and Intersectional Framings of Disability in Comics & Graphic Narratives Edited by Crystal Yin Lie (California State University, Long Beach) and Kai Qing Tan (Ghent University; Aachen University) Comics and graphic narratives have become a powerful medium for representing embodied disability experience. Foundational scholarship in disability and co...
items.fullDescription: Global and Intersectional Framings of Disability in Comics & Graphic Narratives Edited by Crystal Yin Lie (California State University, Long Beach) and Kai Qing Tan (Ghent University; Aachen University) Comics and graphic narratives have become a powerful medium for representing embodied disability experience. Foundational scholarship in disability and comics studies has largely focused on Anglophone and Euro-American contexts. This special issue of Disability Studies Quarterly seeks to expand the field by foregrounding transnational, multilingual, and Global South perspectives. We invite essays that examine how contemporary comics and graphic narratives engage core concepts from disability studies-such as crip time, interdependence and collective access, disability justice, debility and slow death, crip ecologies, and speculative crip futurities-across diverse cultural and geopolitical contexts. Possible topics include (but are not limited to): Representations of physical, sensory, and/or cognitive disability; neurodiversity; chronic illness; aging; and embodied trauma Disability as method/embodied knowledge Resistance to diagnostic and pathological frames Formal strategies that materialize disability in comics form Disability networks, care infrastructures, and collective survival Critiques of social, political, and urban contexts Structural violence, colonialism, environmental crisis, and debility (In)accessibility in both content and form Speculative or futurist imaginaries of disability justice The place of comedy and humor was in disability narratives The commodification of disabilities/narrative via social media and how to circumvent it Ethical obligations in the discourse of disability narratives Theorizations, critiques, and reimaginings of form and genres Emergent directions and movements in comics and comics activism We welcome analyses of transnational and multilingual works, including Japanese manga, South Korean manhwa, Brazilian and Latin American graphic memoir, Indigenous comics, Francophone bandes dessinées from West Africa and the Caribbean, Middle Eastern, Southeast Asian, and Eastern European graphic narratives, graphic journalism, autobiographical comics by disabled artists, and webcomics or other digital formats circulating across borders. In line with our commitment to providing space for disability narratives, we also encourage contributions that combine ethnographic research with creative interventions, integrating praxis alongside more theoretical approaches to the topics listed above. This issue aims to develop comparative methodologies for studying disability and graphic narrative across geographies, languages, and traditions. We encourage submissions from scholars at all career stages and from diverse disciplinary backgrounds. Submission Guidelines: Please submit a paragraph-length (300-500 words) abstract, a list of 3-5 key words, and a brief bio (100 words) by 30 September 2026 (Wed). Invitations to submit full essays (8,000-12,000 words) as well as shorter works of creative writing will follow. Full manuscripts will undergo double-blind peer review in accordance with DSQ guidelines. Send abstracts and inquiries to: Crystal Yin Lie, California State University, Long Beach crystal.lie@csulb.edu Kai Qing Tan, Research Foundation Flanders; Ghent University kaiqing.tan@ugent.be

items.excerpt: Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. This panel invites contributions that examine how food and foodways shape and reflect cultural knowledge across time and place, with particular attention to the relationships among foodways, memory, migration, identity, cultural heritage, and community formation. Pr...
items.fullDescription: Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. This panel invites contributions that examine how food and foodways shape and reflect cultural knowledge across time and place, with particular attention to the relationships among foodways, memory, migration, identity, cultural heritage, and community formation. Proposals are welcome from a range of disciplinary and professional perspectives that explore food and cultural identity in literature, cinema, and other forms of media; oral history projects; mapping initiatives; community archives and digital collections; public-facing scholarship; and pedagogical approaches to Food Studies. The panel also invites discussion of emerging technologies and methodologies that offer new ways of researching, representing, and engaging with foodways and cultural heritage, while expanding opportunities for the communication and exchange of cultural knowledge. Whether through literary and cinematic representation, historical research, oral histories, or other forms of cultural production and inquiry, food-centred scholarship offers valuable insights into the ways individuals and communities understand and communicate culture across generations and geographies. In keeping with the conference theme, Empowering Communities, this session considers how foodways can serve as sites of cultural expression, knowledge-sharing, and collective memory, while encouraging reflection on questions of belonging, exchange, continuity, and cultural change.

items.title: Ecologies of Attention: Literature and Pedagogies of the More-than-Human
items.excerpt: Today's ecological problems demand not only ecological awareness but also a fundamental rethinking of the human-nature relationship with the more-than-human world. Increased climatic change, species extinction, ecological degradation, and planetary instability. Challenge anthropocentric assumptions where humans are seen as separate from, superior to, or s...
items.fullDescription: Today's ecological problems demand not only ecological awareness but also a fundamental rethinking of the human-nature relationship with the more-than-human world. Increased climatic change, species extinction, ecological degradation, and planetary instability. Challenge anthropocentric assumptions where humans are seen as separate from, superior to, or sole interpreters of nature. This panel invites papers that explore how literature, storytelling, environmental humanities, and creative pedagogies might cultivate "ecologies of attention" within teacher education and educational spaces. Rather than approaching nature as a passive object of study or a resource for human learning, the session seeks to examine how literary and cultural texts unsettle human-centered ways of knowing and open possibilities for relational, more-than-human pedagogies. How might literature invite future educators to engage with ecological interconnectedness, multispecies agency, and nonhuman forms of intelligence, communication, and temporality? What pedagogical possibilities emerge when teachers and learners encounter forests, rivers, plants, animals, weather systems, and landscapes not as background settings, but as active participants within shared ecological worlds? How can educational practices move beyond extractive or instrumental approaches to environmental learning toward forms of attentiveness, reciprocity, care, and ethical coexistence? The panel is particularly interested in pedagogical and creative engagements that explore how literary and speculative texts reshape ecological imagination and challenge anthropocentric assumptions within teacher education, curriculum studies, literacy practices, and environmental pedagogy. Possible topics include, but are not limited to: Ecocriticism and teacher education Pedagogies of the more-than-human Literature and ecological attentiveness Climate fiction and environmental pedagogy Multispecies storytelling and relational learning Posthumanism and educational philosophy Ecological imagination in literacy education Biosemiotics, ecological communication, and pedagogy Plant studies, animal studies, and arboreal thinking in education Indigenous ecological knowledge and decolonial pedagogies Eco-poetry, speculative fiction, and climate narratives in the classroom Slow pedagogies, ecological temporality, and relational learning Environmental ethics and pedagogies of coexistence Creative-critical approaches to ecological teaching and learning Affective responses to climate anxiety, grief, and ecological uncertainty The curriculum, sustainability, and more than human relationality are explored. Reading practices that decenter the human subject. Ecological storytelling and pedagogies oriented towards the future. The session invites interdisciplinary, theoretical, pedagogical, creative-critical, and practice-based contributions from scholars, educators, teacher educators, creative writers, and Researchers in the fields of literature, education, environmental humanities, and cultural studies and related fields. Papers engaging works such as The Overstory, How I Became a Tree, The Ministry for the Future, The Grapes of Wrath, Climate Fiction, eco-poems, speculations, and Environmental media are particularly welcome, although not restricted to these texts. Submission Guidelines Please submit: An abstract of 250-300 words A short bio-note (100 words) Institutional affiliation (if applicable) Please send submissions to: mussaratshahid@fccollege.edu.pk

items.title: Call For Papers – NeMLA 2027 Panel -- Supporting the Well-Being of Students and Instructors in a Transitional Time
items.excerpt: Call For Papers - NeMLA 2027 Panel -- Supporting the Well-Being of Students and Instructors in a Transitional Time Northeast MLA 2027: March 6-9, 2027 Panel Title: Supporting the Well-Being of Students and Instructors in a Transitional Time Modality: Virtual Only: All presentations will be delivered via Zoom regardless of whether the presenters are in-per...
items.fullDescription: Call For Papers - NeMLA 2027 Panel -- Supporting the Well-Being of Students and Instructors in a Transitional Time Northeast MLA 2027: March 6-9, 2027 Panel Title: Supporting the Well-Being of Students and Instructors in a Transitional Time Modality: Virtual Only: All presentations will be delivered via Zoom regardless of whether the presenters are in-person Format: 3-4 presentations/papers, 15-20 minutes per presenter The past few years - which could be described as part of a larger cultural, political, and economic flash point -- have created true challenges for higher education. This transitional time has put pressure on all parties of our postsecondary community, as seen in the reported mental health challenges of undergraduates and the high rates of burnout amongst faculty and support networks in the sector (Mowreader; Pourasadollahi and Vincenti; Liu). In addition to these post-pandemic stressors, a host of broader concerns are also impinging on the postsecondary environment (see "The Trends Report" for one discussion). How can we empower our community - defined through instructors, students, and/or support services -- during such a transitional moment? This session will create a dialogue that touches on any of the following: -discussions/studies of interventions and/or articulations of already established practices in supporting the well-being of students and/or instructors; -the reflections of students, instructors, and/or support networks on the challenges of and responses to our contemporary moment. These challenges can include: the impact of budget cuts on postsecondary contexts, the fallout of declining academic freedom in the classroom, the impact (positive or negative) of Gen AI on the well-being of our community, the discord between instructor expectations and the reality of students. References Liu, Adrienne. "Higher Education is Exhausted." The Chronicle of Higher Education, 10 Feb. 2025, https://www.chronicle.com/article/higher-education-is-exhausted. Mowreader, Ashley. "Survey: Getting a Grip on the Student Mental Health Crisis," Inside Higher Ed, 19 Aug. 2024, https://www.insidehighered.com/news/student-success/health-wellness/2024/08/19/experts-weigh-college-student-mental-health-crisis. Pourasadollahi, Khatereh and Michele Vincenti. "Unveiling the Layers of Faculty Burnout in Canadian Private Universities during the post-COVID Pandemic Period." Journal of Education and Learning, vol. 14, no. 2, 2025, pp. 43-52. https://doi.org/10.5539/jel.v14n2p43. "The Trends Report 2026," The Chronicle of Higher Education, March 2026, https://www.chronicle.com/package/trends-report-2026. Submission Details 200-300 word abstract to the NeMLA portal (https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22639) by September 30, 2026 Please email any questions to Stacy Denton (sdenton@uwaterloo.ca)

items.location: Newport, RI - Hybrid · Salve Regina University
items.excerpt: NeMLA 2027 March 6-9, 2027 Newport, RI Salve Regina University Southern Italy beyond stereotypes and exoticization (Roundtable, Hybrid) CFP https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22680 Southern Italy is often depicted stereotypically, both in Italian cultural production and in international social media content and blogs that promote La Dolce Vita as a desirabl...
items.fullDescription: NeMLA 2027 March 6-9, 2027 Newport, RI Salve Regina University Southern Italy beyond stereotypes and exoticization (Roundtable, Hybrid) CFP https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22680 Southern Italy is often depicted stereotypically, both in Italian cultural production and in international social media content and blogs that promote La Dolce Vita as a desirable lifestyle. While instructors can leverage some stereotypes and content in in-class activities to teach Italian language and culture, lower the affective filter, or introduce new topics and discussions, an excessive use of reels, songs, skits, films, and literature that play with such generalizations runs the risk of promoting a backward or distorted view of the country, its culture, and its communities, already affected by prejudice, overtourism and the touristification of entire neighborhoods or cities. In keeping with this year's conference theme, this roundtable invites short (5-10 minutes) presentations that explore ideas and content that convey the complexity of Italian culture and society beyond the stereotypes and the exoticization of Southern Italy, its cultures, places, communities, and bodies. Presentations may focus on pedagogical aspects, activities, and practices, or on primary sources used in the L2/LS and content classrooms to discuss any aspect of Italian culture and society without focusing on clichés related to the South (including Sicily, Sardinia, and the Mediterranean). Please submit a 200- 250-word abstract and a short bio through the NeMLA website (CFP https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22680) by September 30, 2026. For questions, please contact the roundtable organizers: Giuseppina Gemboni (ggemboni@andrew.cmu.edu), Angela Fabris (Angela.Fabris@aau.at).
