items.title: Teaching Trans Literature
items.excerpt: It's been twenty years since Susan Stryker's groundbreaking The Transgender Studies Reader, which not only helped scholars across disciplines teach themselves the background to responsibly teach Trans Studies but also provided easily excerpt-able essays that were highly teachable. Twenty years ago, however, literature by and about trans individuals was li...
items.fullDescription: It's been twenty years since Susan Stryker's groundbreaking The Transgender Studies Reader, which not only helped scholars across disciplines teach themselves the background to responsibly teach Trans Studies but also provided easily excerpt-able essays that were highly teachable. Twenty years ago, however, literature by and about trans individuals was limited almost entirely to autobiographies - an invaluable genre, of course, but limiting for literary scholars and instructors who also wanted to assign more formally complex literary forms that tackle these same themes (Stryker's volume does include essays on creative texts but they are all about visual media). My hope for this panel is that panelists bring their one or two favorite literary trans texts *to teach* -poetry, fiction, and plays. What kind of course do you include it in? What kind of students take this course? How have they responded to your chosen text(s)? What kinds of strategies do you use to teach the text, and/or what kind of writing assignments accompany the text(s) on the syllabus? Ideally this panel would also then become a destination for other teacher/scholars at the annual conference who want to add diversity to what they assign but don't have time to start from scratch. Panel is IN-PERSON. Guidelines for Paper Abstracts ( must be submitted to NeMLA link included in this CFP; please do not email abstracts (though you're welcome to email questions ). Anyone may submit an individual abstract to a particular session. Abstracts are vetted by the session chairs. Membership is not required to submit an abstract, but if your abstract is accepted and you want to participate, you must A) become a member, and then B) register for the convention. Submissions of paper abstracts are accepted from June 16 through September 30, and session chairs will confirm the acceptance of abstracts by October 16. Once notified, please confirm your participation with chairs by accepting their invitations. Each abstract submission must include: *A title of no more than 80 characters *An abstract of 200 to 300 words *A brief bio

Drawing on Cultural Studies, feminist, queer, posthuman, and decolonial frameworks, it highlights speculative narratives as tools for interrogating late capitalism, technological mediat...
items.fullDescription: This panel explores speculative fiction in US culture as a critical lens for examining power, history, and futurity across literature, film, television, and digital media. Drawing on Cultural Studies, feminist, queer, posthuman, and decolonial frameworks, it highlights speculative narratives as tools for interrogating late capitalism, technological mediation, racialized and gendered subjectivities, and ecological crises. Emphasizing counter-hegemonic futures-through Afrofuturism, Indigenous and Latinx futurisms, and minoritarian practices-this panel considers how speculative texts reimagine temporalities, ontologies, and modes of resistance. We aim to foster an interdisciplinary dialogue on alternative futures and cultural critique. Suggested Topics for Proposals: We invite contributions on a wide range of media and narratives, including but not limited to: Posthumanism and Cyborg Ontologies: Redefining the boundaries of the human, machine integration, and non-human agency. The Eerie and Speculative Horror: Uncanny spaces, Hauntology, and the architectural or psychological eerie in US cultural memory. Alternative and Minoritarian Futurisms: Counter-hegemonic narratives through Afrofuturism, Indigenous futurisms and Latinx futurisms. Climate Fiction (Cli-Fi) and Ecological Crises: Anthropocene narratives, environmental decay, and post-apocalyptic landscapes. Utopian and Dystopian Imaginaries: Surveillance, late capitalism, and systemic resistance. Queer Futurities: Disrupting heteronormative temporalities and imagining alternative spaces of belonging. Transmedia Storytelling and Participatory World-Building: Digital media, video games, and collaborative speculative universes. Submission Guidelines: Proposals must be submitted directly to the panel organizers using the official SAAS paper proposal form. Download the form here: SAAS Paper Proposal Form https://saas-web.org/saas-oviedo-2027/ Send your completed form via email to BOTH panel chairs: Elena Romea Parente: eromea@hum.uc3m.es Carmen Gómez Galisteo: cgomez@flog.uned.es Important Dates: Submission Deadline: October 15, 2026 (Note: While the conference takes place in March 2027, academic CFP deadlines traditionally close the preceding autumn). Conference Dates: March 15, 16, and 17, 2027

items.fullDescription: Polish Science Fiction: Literature, Film, and Cultural Legacy Despite the international prominence of Stanis?aw Lem and, more recently, Andrzej Sapkowski's The Witcher, Polish science fiction and speculative literature remain strikingly underrepresented in English-language scholarship. Existing criticism tends to orbit a small number of canonical figures and a handful of frequently revisited texts, leaving a rich, historically complex tradition, spanning censorship-era political allegory, post-1989 transformation, contemporary literary crossover, and a distinct national cinema largely unexamined outside of Polish-language scholarship. This edited collection seeks to address that gap. We invite chapter proposals that expand the scope of English-language engagement with Polish speculative fiction and film: introducing under-translated authors, revisiting canonical figures through new critical lenses, and treating Polish science-fiction cinema as a subject rather than solely through the lens of a single adaptation. We welcome contributions from literary scholars, film scholars, translation studies specialists, and researchers in Slavic and Central European studies. We are especially interested in proposals that · move beyond Lem and Sapkowski as the sole representatives of the tradition. - Bring new theoretical frameworks (postcolonial theory, translation studies, media studies, gender studies, posthumanism) to bear on Polish speculative texts - Address film and other media as legitimate objects of study, not only as adaptations of literary source material - Situate Polish SF within broader Central/Eastern European or global speculative fiction discourse Possible topics include, but are not limited to: Historical Foundations: Science fiction under the Polish People's Republic Censorship, allegory, and political subtext in Cold War-era SF Fandom, zines, and early SF publishing culture in Poland Lem Beyond Solaris Underexamined works: (e.g. The Cyberiad, His Master's Voice, Fiasco, Return from the Stars) Lem's philosophy of science, epistemology, and technology Lem as critic and theorist of the genre (Fantastyka i futurologia) Post-1989 and Contemporary Voices Jacek Dukaj, Jaros?aw Grz?dowicz, Marek Huberath, and other contemporary authors Speculative elements in literary/crossover fiction (e.g. Olga Tokarczuk) Polish fantasy and its relationship to the SF tradition (including Sapkowski) Translation, Circulation, and Reception The politics and practice of translating Polish SF into English Publishing history: which authors and works reach Anglophone readers, and why Comparative reception of Polish SF in the West versus domestically Polish Science Fiction on Screen Polish-made dystopian and speculative cinema (e.g. Piotr Szulkin's tetralogy, Andrzej ?u?awski) Adaptation studies: Lem's Solaris and its cinematic afterlives, approached through fresh angles (production politics, reception history, or contemporary resonance) rather than as a straightforward novel-versus-film comparison Polish SF's influence on or intersection with games and other media (e.g. CD Projekt Red) Send an abstract of 300-500 words and a short (150-word max) academic biography by October 15, 2026, to Dr. Agnieszka Gutthy at agutthy@selu.edu When accepted, full chapter (5000-8000 words including notes and bibliography) will be due May 15, 2027.

items.excerpt: TRANSPOSITIONES Journal for Transdisciplinary and Intermedial Culture Studies / Zeitschrift fu?r transdisziplinäre und intermediale Kulturforschung, ISSN 2749-4128 (print), 2749-4136 (online), Vol. 6, No. 2 (2027) Call for Papers Thematic focus of the issue: Earth / Sediment / Trace: Geologies, Material Memories, and the More-than-Living World Across the...
items.fullDescription: TRANSPOSITIONES Journal for Transdisciplinary and Intermedial Culture Studies / Zeitschrift fu?r transdisziplinäre und intermediale Kulturforschung, ISSN 2749-4128 (print), 2749-4136 (online), Vol. 6, No. 2 (2027) Call for Papers Thematic focus of the issue: Earth / Sediment / Trace: Geologies, Material Memories, and the More-than-Living World Across the environmental humanities, increasing attention has been devoted to the geological dimensions of culture and the cultural dimensions of the Earth. Geohumanities, critical mineral studies, energy humanities, environmental justice scholarship, and related fields have opened new perspectives on extraction, sedimentation, toxicity, waste, material memory, and deep time. At a moment marked by accelerating ecological transformations and growing awareness of the material infrastructures that sustain contemporary life, geological matter has emerged as a crucial site for rethinking history, memory, environment, and cultural imagination. Rather than treating the Earth as a passive backdrop to human activity, recent scholarship has approached rocks, minerals, soils, fossils, sediments, and other material formations as archives of planetary processes, environmental change, cultural memory, and political conflict. Questions of resource extraction, environmental justice, and slow violence increasingly intersect with inquiries into deep temporalities, material traces, sacred landscapes, and the long afterlives of human and more-than-human actions. This issue invites contributions that explore how literature, art, media, philosophy, religion, and cultural practices engage with geological matter, deep time, sedimented histories, and the material conditions of planetary life. We especially welcome work that investigates the cultural significance of rocks, minerals, fossils, soil, waste, ruins, and other forms of more-than-living matter, while also addressing questions of environmental justice, resource extraction, toxicity, and slow violence. We welcome analyses of literary, philosophical, historical, and cultural texts from a wide range of regions and traditions, including comparative and transnational perspectives. Topics may include: • geohumanities and geological imagination; • earth, stone, sediment, dust, and soil; • lithic ecologies and critical mineral studies; • deep time, planetary temporalities, and environmental history; • extractivism, mining, resource conflicts, and energy humanities; • environmental justice and slow violence; • toxic landscapes and contaminated environments; • waste, ruins, landfills, and material afterlives; • fossils, paleontology, and material histories of life; • material memory, archives, traces, and sedimented histories; • sacred geographies, holy mountains, stones, and pilgrimage landscapes; • archaeology, antiquarianism, and the materialities of the past; • textual strata, sedimented knowledge, and epistemic layers; • geological trauma, memory, and post-catastrophic landscapes; • petrofiction, climate fiction, and environmental storytelling; • artistic and literary engagements with earth materials; • relations between living and non-living matter; • fungi, decomposition, and processes of material transformation; • posthumanist, new materialist, and decolonial approaches to geology and the earth. Proposals comprising a 250-word abstract in English or German and a brief biographical note should be sent to: transpositiones@uw.edu.pl by October 20, 2026. A final decision regarding abstract acceptance will be made by October 31. Deadline for submitting completed manuscripts: February 28, 2027. The issue is expected to be published in the autumn of 2027.

items.fullDescription: Call for Chapters Proposals Submission Deadline: July 17, 2026 Full Chapters Due: October 30, 2026 Submission Date: October 30, 2026 Introduction This edited collection features scholarly voices with attention to horror in its transmedia representations, including film, literature, comics, art, branding, and more. Chapters give attention to the ways that fans take up the realities and ideas presented in horror, the affordances of the genre, and participation in this network of storytelling. The reactions of readers/viewers/fans, the decisions of creatives, the presentation of ideas within particular forms of media, and more form elements of the researched conversation in this volume. Objective This collection seeks to advance conversation about the theoretical functions of horror across media including notions of adaptation and literary/literacy-based choices in the work. This includes, but is not limited to, design structures, cultural influences, the role of lore, (re)considerations of the monstrous, embodiment in the genre, sub-genre considerations, and more. Target Audience This book is intended for academics in media, literacy, literature, communication, and interdisciplinary fields. Students enrolled in courses in these fields can benefit from this knowledge as they pursue courses of study and further academic work in these disciplines. Recommended Topics Emotional and Philosophical Reactions to Horror Media Themes and Interactions across Media Representations in the Genre Adaptation Processes/Decisions/Designs in Horror across Media Studies of Narrative and Response/Interpretation Recent Trends/Movements in Horror Theoretical Approaches to Horror Composition/Representation Analysis of Race, Gender, Class in Horror Considerations and Reconsiderations of the Monstrous Author/Creator Decisions and Purposes in the Genre/across Media Horror Character and Author/Director Studies Critical and Analytical Work on Embodiment in Horror Critical and Analytical Examinations of Cultural Stories in the Genre The Role of Lore/Mythology in Horror Studies of Writing/Creating Processes in the Genre Fandom Communities, Fan Fiction, Cosplay, and Other Responses Submission Procedure Researchers and practitioners are invited to submit on or before July 17, 2026, a chapter proposal of 1,000 to 2,000 words clearly explaining the mission and concerns of his or her proposed chapter. Authors will be notified by July 31, 2026 about the status of their proposals and sent chapter guidelines.Full chapters of a minimum of 10,000 words (word count includes references and related readings) are expected to be submitted by October 30, 2026, and all interested authors must consult the guidelines for manuscript submissions at https://www.igi-global.com/publish/contributor-resources/before-you-write/ prior to submission. All submitted chapters will be reviewed on a double-anonymized review basis. Contributors may also be requested to serve as reviewers for this project. Note: There are no submission or acceptance fees for manuscripts submitted to this book publication, Theory and Practice in Horror Transmedia Fandom and Creation. All manuscripts are accepted based on a double-anonymized peer review editorial process. All proposals should be submitted through the eEditorial Discovery® online submission manager. Publisher This book is scheduled to be published by IGI Global Scientific Publishing, an international academic publisher of the "Information Science Reference", "Medical Information Science Reference", "Business Science Reference", and "Engineering Science Reference" imprints. IGI Global Scientific Publishing specializes in publishing reference books, scholarly journals, and electronic databases featuring academic research on a variety of innovative topic areas including, but not limited to, education, social science, medicine and healthcare, business and management, information science and technology, engineering, public administration, library and information science, media and communication studies, and environmental science. For additional information regarding the publisher, please visit https://www.igi-global.com. This publication is anticipated to be released in 2027. Indexing Information for Prospective Authors IGI Global Scientific Publishing meets the criteria for inclusion in major indexing services such as Scopus; however, it is important to note that all indexing decisions are made independently by these services. IGI Global Scientific Publishing books are selectively indexed by the indexing organization after publication. Indexing cannot be guaranteed for any book prior to publication, and the indexing organization has complete control over the final selection and timeline. Important Dates July 17, 2026: Proposal Submission Deadline July 31, 2026: Notification of Acceptance October 30, 2026: Full Chapter Submission January 1, 2027: Review Results Returned February 12, 2027: Final Acceptance Notification February 26, 2027: Final Chapter Submission Inquiries Jason DeHart University of Tennessee Knoxville dehartjason33@gmail.com Classifications Education; Library and Information Science; Media and Communications; Social Sciences and Humanities

items.title: New Approaches to Teaching the Gothic
items.excerpt: Diane Long Hoeveler and Tamar Heller's Approaches to Teaching Gothic Fiction: British and American Traditions (MLA Options for Teaching) was published in 2003; Anna Powell and Andrew Smith's Teaching the Gothic (Palgrave) was published in 2006. While both texts are excellent guides for teachers of the Gothic, quite a lot has happened in the twenty years s...
items.fullDescription: Diane Long Hoeveler and Tamar Heller's Approaches to Teaching Gothic Fiction: British and American Traditions (MLA Options for Teaching) was published in 2003; Anna Powell and Andrew Smith's Teaching the Gothic (Palgrave) was published in 2006. While both texts are excellent guides for teachers of the Gothic, quite a lot has happened in the twenty years since these texts appeared, not the least of which was a Gothic-infused global pandemic and the insidious rise of artificial intelligence in daily life. Academics today are and have been teaching the Gothic at a time when the world's problems - rising fascism, global conflict, climate change, the devaluation of Higher Education, among many others - make the world itself seem downright Gothic. This collection will seek to provide updated reflections, evaluations, and strategies on teaching the Gothic and utilising or creating Gothic pedagogy in the contemporary moment and beyond. Courses on or including the Gothic are taught in a wide variety of venues within and outside academia; settings include community colleges, traditional undergraduate programmes, graduate programmes, community centres, prisons and reformatories. Classrooms themselves vary greatly, from giant lecture halls to small upper-level seminars to fully online virtual spaces. Students of the Gothic come from all ages and backgrounds, and include native speakers of English and students acquiring English later in life. These contexts, venues, and audiences present different challenges for the instructor, and require unique approaches to make for successful learning experiences. We are seeking contributions from a range of scholars at different ranks and types of institutions (as well as outside of them) to present a broad understanding of the diversity of how we study and teach the Gothic today, and to provide new directions for the future. We already have interest from an academic press. Potential topics might include but are not restricted to one or a mixture of the following: Teaching the Gothic in the midst of a crisis (e.g. pandemic, war, from a distance or physically within such spaces) Teaching the Gothic outside of the university / in non-traditional settings Challenges of AI to teaching the Gothic The use of various digital platforms in teaching / student research Gothic and the archives in contemporary times What texts (and which editions) absolutely must be on any Gothic syllabus (and why)? Gothic forms (teaching beyond traditional formats, for instance) Gothic theory as pedagogy (in times of crisis particularly) Syllabus organizational strategies and rationales: Chronological? Thematic? Canon? Decolonizing the Gothic syllabus Preparing for and managing class discussions about violence, sexual violence, trauma, discrimination, or other tough topics that arise in the Gothic? Strategies for teaching a Gothic course for an audience unfamiliar with the Gothic / unfamiliar with academia / non-traditional students. Gothic and public engagement / school outreach Opportunities for service learning / personal and professional development in the Gothic course Creative forms of assessment in the Gothic classroom Neurodivergence and the Gothic Designing assignments with AI in mind Cancel culture and the Gothic Co-editors Bridget Marshall (University of Massachusetts, Lowell) and Jen Baker (University of Warwick, UK) seek abstracts of 300-500 words for chapters which will demonstrate innovative approaches to teaching the Gothic or engaging with or creating new Gothic pedagogies, particularly situated within recent contexts. We are open to a variety of forms, including case studies, reflective essays, theoretically informed essays, and other formats that can best showcase pedagogical insights. If your proposed essay includes samples of work by students/participants we will require written permissions before publication. Please also include a brief professional bio of 300 words (max) with your abstract. Please send your abstract and bio by 10 November 2026 to Bridget_Marshall@uml.edu and J.Baker.5@warwick.ac.uk We are happy to answer questions at the same address.

items.fullDescription: Call for Papers - Volume 37, Issue 1 & 2 (2027) Dear Colleagues, Litera: Journal of Language, Literature and Culture Studies - Dil, Edebiyat ve Kültür Ara?t?rmalar? Dergisi, the official publication of the Department of Western Languages and Literatures at Istanbul University, invites submissions for its 2026 issues (Volume 36, Issues 1 & 2). An open-access, peer-reviewed, and multilingual journal published twice a year (June & December), Litera is indexed in ELSEVIER SCOPUS, Web of Science ESCI, MLA International Bibliography, DOAJ (Directory of Open Access Journals), ERIH PLUS, EBSCO Communication Source, EBSCO Central & Eastern European Academic Source, Gale Cengage and Ulrichsweb. While our core focus remains on literary studies, linguistics, and cultural studies, we welcome interdisciplinary approaches that combine literary and linguistic analysis with insights from cultural theory and media studies-provided they maintain a clear relevance to our aim and scope We welcome original research articles that engage with current debates and critical issues in the study of Western (American, English, French, Belgian, German, Swiss, Austrian, Italian, Spanish, Latin American and Modern Greek) languages, literatures, and cultures. In particular, we encourage contributions that reflect cutting-edge scholarship in areas such as, but not limited to: · Contemporary literary theory · Disability Studies · Human-Animal Studies · Posthumanism and critical posthumanities · Ecofeminism and ecocriticism · Ecosemiotics and Ecolinguistics · Discourse Studies · Intersectional and decolonial approaches · Digital humanities and media theory · Film Studies · Medieval Studies · Posthuman Communication Studies · Gender studies · Narrative and identity in contemporary fiction · Transnationalism and migration literature · Crete studies Submission Windows: · Issue 1 (June 2027): November 1-30, 2026 · Issue 2 (December 2027): May 1-31, 2027 Submission Guidelines: Manuscripts must be original, unpublished, and not under review elsewhere. Re-use of thesis content, dissertations, or previously published conference material is not permitted. All submissions are subject to a double-blind peer reviewprocess. Languages of Publication: English, German, French, Italian, and Spanish No processing or publication fees. Eligibility: Submissions are accepted from authors who hold a PhD. For more information, visit our journal homepage. We look forward to receiving your innovative and thought-provoking research. Sincerely, Editorial Team Litera: Journal of Language, Literature and Culture Studies

items.fullDescription: Journal of Studies in Language, Culture and Society (JSLCS) ISSN / E-ISSN: 2716-9189 / 2676-1750 Publisher: University of Bejaia Editor-in-chief: Prof. Nadia Idri, Author Guidelines for JSLCS Standards Our Goal: To be a leading international, multidisciplinary forum for high-quality research. To achieve this, we require your active partnership in building a rigorous, visible, and influential scholarly community. 1. Core Philosophy: Quality, Originality, and International Contribution To be considered, your manuscript must make a clear, original, and significant contribution to its field on an international level. We prioritize research that engages with global scholarly conversations, not just regional or national discussions. Ask yourself: Why would a scholar on another continent find this paper important? 2. Abstract: Your International Showcase The abstract is your most critical tool for attracting an international readership. Length & Detail: Provide a structured abstract of 300-350 words. Use clear headings: Objective, Methodology, Results, Conclusion, Significance/Contribution. Content: Explicitly state the paper's novelty and its significance to the international research community. Avoid generic statements. Specify the gap you fill and how your findings advance the field globally. Language: Must be in excellent, native-level English. Consider professional editing if needed. This is non-negotiable for discoverability. 3. Manuscript Scope & "The Identity Challenge" JSLCS is multidisciplinary, but your paper must have a sharp, focused identity. Connect to Core Themes: While we cover language, culture, and society, your manuscript must clearly fit within one or two defined sub-fields. Justify Broad Relevance: If your topic is regionally specific (e.g., a case study in Algeria), the Introduction and Conclusion must explicitly articulate its theoretical, methodological, or comparative implications for international research. Avoid being "a paper of everything": We will desk-reject manuscripts that are overly broad, lack a clear theoretical framework, or do not connect to specific scholarly debates. 4. References & Engagement with the Global Scholarly Community Your reference list is a map of your scholarly engagement and is crucial for our global profile. Volume & Recency: A minimum of 25-30 high-quality references is expected. A significant majority should be from the last 5 years, demonstrating engagement with current discourse. Source Quality: Prioritize references from Scopus/Web of Science-indexed journals and reputable international academic presses (Routledge, Springer, Palgrave, John Benjamins, etc.). International Dialogue: Your literature review must engage with key international scholars and seminal works in your field, not just literature from a single region. Style: Strict adherence to APA 7th Edition. 5. Authorship & Affiliation International Collaboration: Research conducted by international teams or comparing multiple contexts is highly encouraged. Institutional Email & ORCID: All corresponding authors must provide an institutional email address and a valid ORCID ID. This is mandatory for submission. Author Profiles: Authors are encouraged to maintain active, public scholarly profiles (e.g., Google Scholar, Scopus Author ID, institutional webpage). 6. Language and Accessibility for an International Audience The full manuscript must be submitted in English. To enhance accessibility, we recommend that authors of highly regionally-focused studies consider including an extended summary (500-600 words) in the article's appendix, detailing the context and international relevance for non-specialists. 7. Ethical Rigor & Transparency AI Use Disclosure: Any use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools in the writing, editing, or analysis process must be transparently declared in the methodology or acknowledgments section. Describe the tool used and its purpose. AI cannot be listed as an author. Plagiarism Check: All submissions will be screened. Similarity must be below 20% (excluding references). Ethical Compliance: Studies involving humans must include proof of ethics approval. Submission Checklist: Before submitting via ASJP, ensure your manuscript: Makes a clear international contribution. Has a detailed, structured abstract highlighting novelty and significance. Fits a defined niche within our broad scope. Contains 25-30+ references (can be more), mostly recent and from high-indexed sources. Engages with international literature. Is written in excellent, polished English. Uses the correct template and APA 7th style. Includes author ORCID iD and institutional email. Has a declaration on AI use (if applicable). Submission via ASJP https://asjp.cerist.dz/en/PresentationRevue/681 More information: https://univ-bejaia.dz/jslcs

items.excerpt: The Southern Humanities Conference, 2027 Call for Papers Conference Theme: Divergence, Convergence, Emergence Knoxville, TN, January 28-31, 2027 The Southern Humanities Conference offers an opportunity for scholars, artists, writers, musicians, performers, and humanists of all kinds to share their knowledge, research, work, and experiences in an interdisc...
items.fullDescription: The Southern Humanities Conference, 2027 Call for Papers Conference Theme: Divergence, Convergence, Emergence Knoxville, TN, January 28-31, 2027 The Southern Humanities Conference offers an opportunity for scholars, artists, writers, musicians, performers, and humanists of all kinds to share their knowledge, research, work, and experiences in an interdisciplinary, welcoming, and engaging intellectual space. For 2027 our conference theme will be "Divergence, Convergence, Emergence." Divergent thinking is a hallmark of creative endeavors ranging from the visual and performative arts to scientific inquiry. It can also signify a transgression of a custom or standard, or even a social more or behavioral norm, through contravention or subversion of what others consider "ordinary" or "natural." On the other hand, the benefits of group diversity are undeniably numerous and valuable in education, collaboration, and problem-solving. Convergent thinking can help triangulate resolutions to mysteries in historical systems and literary plot structures, among other questions. Convergence can also be considered a precursor to meaningful communal and cooperative social and cultural interactions. The notion of emergence-a phenomenon in which novel, unpredictable, and sometimes unexpected outcomes arise from a constellation of interactions between simpler underlying elements-provides a way of understanding origins and evolutions of phenomena that interest the analytical and imaginative mind. Emergence is also often associated with the concept of synthesis and the idea that the whole can be greater than the sum of its parts. The intersections of (and interstices between) emergence, divergence, and convergence serve as the stimulus for our collective conversation in Knoxville and offers us a platform for appraising the world around us. The Southern Humanities Conference invites proposals for papers on any aspect of the theme "Divergence, Convergence, Emergence" broadly conceived. Our conference themes are meant to be inspiring and prompt reflection, not limiting. The topic is interdisciplinary and invites proposals from all areas of study, as well as creative pieces including but not limited to performance, music, art, and literature. Customary paper and full panel proposals are invited, as are ones for creative presentation formats like roundtables and demonstrations. Moreover, the Southern Humanities Conference welcomes proposals from teachers and professionals outside the academy, as well as from scholars in the early stages of their academic careers. Please note that the name of our organization simply reflects its having been founded in the U.S. South; no presenter is expected to present anything "southern," though southern topics are also welcomed. Conference attendees come from all over the United States, Canada, and overseas. SUBMISSION GUIDELINES: Please submit proposals of 300-500 words through our website at www.southernhumanities.org (preferred), or by email sent to Brett Bebber at southernhumanities@gmail.com. Proposals are due by December 1, 2026, but are accepted and reviewed on a rolling basis. We are currently planning to meet for an in-person conference in Knoxville. Conference registration fees for the 2027 Conference will be $195 for students and retirees and $250 for academic faculty and other professionals. CONFERENCE AWARDS: Two awards of up to $300 are available to help mitigate the costs of travel to the conference. The Kathryn M. Reynolds award is for first- or second-time presenters, and Bennie D. Ussery award is for the best proposed paper from a graduate student. A third award, the Mark Ledbetter and Susan Cumings Award, offers registration remission for up to four graduate students. If you would like to be considered for one of these awards, please indicate which award(s) in your proposal. Those interested in being considered for either award should submit their proposals by October 31, 2026.

items.fullDescription: Digital Social Sciences (Volume 3, Issue 2 (2026) Digital Social Sciences (ISSN: 3078-4980 (Online), 3078-4972 (Print) is an international, peer-reviewed, open-access journal dedicated to advancing interdisciplinary research on the transformative impact of digital technologies on society. The journal provides a global platform for scholars, researchers, policymakers, and practitioners to examine the opportunities, challenges, and implications of digital transformation across the social sciences. The Editorial Board invites original, unpublished, and high-quality manuscripts for Volume 3, Issue 2 (2026). Themes of Interest Submissions are invited in, but are not limited to, the following areas: Artificial Intelligence and Society Digital Governance and Public Policy Data Privacy and Cyber Law Digital Rights and Human Rights Digital Economy and Platform Capitalism Social Media, Politics, and Democracy Misinformation, Disinformation, and Digital Literacy Big Data and Computational Social Science Digital Sociology Digital Anthropology Digital Humanities E-Government and Digital Public Services Educational Technology and Digital Learning Digital Communication and Media Studies Digital Health and Telemedicine Smart Cities and Urban Innovation Algorithmic Governance and Ethics Digital Inequality and Social Inclusion Surveillance, Privacy, and Data Ethics FinTech and Digital Financial Inclusion Blockchain and Society Internet Governance Cybersecurity Policy Online Communities and Digital Culture Climate Technologies and Sustainable Digital Development Human-Computer Interaction Digital Research Methods Emerging Technologies and Society Interdisciplinary Digital Social Science Research Types of Manuscripts The journal welcomes: Original Research Articles Review Articles Systematic Literature Reviews Short Communications Policy Papers Case Studies Conceptual Papers Book Reviews Why Publish with DSS? Double-Blind Peer Review Fully Open Access International Editorial Board DOI Assignment for Every Published Article Rapid Editorial and Peer Review Process Global Visibility and Academic Reach Immediate Online Publication upon Acceptance Creative Commons Licensing No Submission Fee Submission Guidelines Authors should ensure that: The manuscript is original and has not been published or submitted elsewhere. items.fullDescription: The International Journal of English Literature, Social Sciences, and Multidisciplinary Studies (IJELS) publishes original research papers, review articles, and case studies that advance scholarly knowledge across English literature, social sciences, and multidisciplinary studies. The journal provides a global platform for researchers, educators, and professionals to share innovative ideas, critical analyses, and interdisciplinary perspectives. Submission open to Current Issue Feedback Notification: within 1 Month after submission (Online Peer Review System) IJELS welcomes high-quality submissions on a broad range of topics, including but not limited to: English Literature and Language Studies - English literature, world literature, comparative literature, interdisciplinary approaches to literature, literature and media, critical theory, English language teaching and learning, communicative language teaching (CLT), task-based language teaching (TBLT), second language acquisition, English language testing and assessment, syllabus design and curriculum development, applied linguistics, corpus linguistics, discourse analysis, translation studies, English globalization, and English-speaking cultures. Social Sciences and Humanities - Anthropology, Sociology, Psychology, Political Science, Economics, Education, Geography, History, Law, International Relations, Criminology, Public Administration, Development Studies, Human Rights, Social Welfare, Gender Studies, and Ethics. Arts and Cultural Studies - Performing Arts (music, theatre, and dance), Visual Arts, Cultural and Cross-Cultural Studies, Media Studies, Communication Studies, and Philosophy. Business, Law, and Information Studies - Business Studies, Corporate Governance, Industrial Relations, Information Science, Library Science, and Paralegal Studies. Multidisciplinary Studies - Research that bridges multiple academic disciplines to address complex social, cultural, linguistic, educational, technological, or global challenges. IJELS particularly values cross-cutting studies that integrate methodologies and perspectives from different fields to generate new insights and foster innovation. IJELS encourages diverse theoretical, empirical, and methodological approaches that promote intellectual exchange and interdisciplinary collaboration, fostering research at the dynamic intersection of literature, language, social sciences, and multidisciplinary inquiry. You may submit your article to: ijels.infogain@gmail.com

items.title: The How-To Issue
items.excerpt: Public Humanities is a new international open access, cross-disciplinary, peer-reviewed journal at the intersection of humanities scholarship and public life. The journal invites proposals for themed issues that pose urgent questions on contemporary public issues that require rigorous and relevant humanities knowledge. The journal invites submissions to i...
items.fullDescription: Public Humanities is a new international open access, cross-disciplinary, peer-reviewed journal at the intersection of humanities scholarship and public life. The journal invites proposals for themed issues that pose urgent questions on contemporary public issues that require rigorous and relevant humanities knowledge. The journal invites submissions to its upcoming Themed Issue 'The How-To Issue', which will be edited by Jeffrey R. Wilson. Deadline for submissions 1 January 2027. Description Public humanities often require an expert in one area to learn another field or skill on-the-fly or to collaborate effectively across boundaries of specialization. Especially for younger thinkers and doers, fear of the unknown combines with taken-for-granted assumptions about standard forms of humanities work to prevent our pursuit of promising public humanities projects and careers. Even veteran humanities scholars who have extensive expertise in areas vitally important to public life can crumble when confronted with the prospect of moving outside their comfort zone of academic protocols to engage with audiences via new formats or professional pathways. This issue invites practical guidance on doing public humanities. That work often involves meeting audiences where they are via media, formats, and professions that require (1) conceptual understanding of what these venues offer and (2) technical training in the nuts-and-bolts of best practices. The How-To Issue will feature a series of case studies, reflections and essays by people who have had success in a variety of formats related to public humanities (e.g., museums, activism, podcasts, policy work, etc.) offering critical reflection and guidance to people in the next generation who might be interested in those pathways but aren't really sure where to start or how to do it. From public writing to social media, from graduate program administration to empirical research, from fundraising to publishing, the issue will provide concrete case studies of successes, failures, lessons learned the hard way, and time-tested strategies for turning ideas into realities. The editor invites papers covering any form of public humanities, including but not limited to: Activism Blogs Book Clubs Community Conversations Cultural Organizations Educational Departments Empirical Studies Evaluation Funding and fundraising Grants Libraries Mass Media Multimedia Formats Museums Podcasts Policy Practice-Based Research Program Administration Public Programs and Events Public Schools (Pre-College) Public Writing Researching the Public Humanities Social Media Websites Submissions can include any or all of the follow article types: Article type? Length? Abstract required? Description? Reflection 1,500-4,000 words No Theorizing the affordances and impact of a format or profession. Case Study 2,000-4,000 words Yes An article that provides an in-depth, detailed examination of a particular case and narrates challenges, successes, failures, and lessons learned. Essay 1,500-4,000 No A discussion paper that reflects critically on a particular form of public humanities. Submission guidelines Submissions should be written in accessible language for a wide readership across and beyond the humanities. Articles will be peer reviewed for both content and style. Articles will appear digitally and open access in the journal.? All submissions should be made through the Public Humanities online peer review system. Author should consult the journal's Author Instructions prior to submission.? All authors will be required to declare any funding and/or competing interests upon submission. See the journal's Publishing Ethics guidelines for more information.?? Contacts Guest Editor name: Jeffrey R. Wilson Email: publichumanities@cambridge.org Questions regarding peer review can also be sent to the Public Humanities inbox at publichumanities@cambridge.org Learn more at cambridge.org/pub

items.location: online · "L&D initiatives" Nataliia Gach Education Centre
items.excerpt: Our conference is a global online gathering of educators, instructional designers, learning innovators, researchers, and industry leaders dedicated to shaping the future of learning. LXD has emerged as a powerful bridge between learning design and educational technology, combining pedagogical expertise with technological innovation to create engaging, lea...
items.fullDescription: Our conference is a global online gathering of educators, instructional designers, learning innovators, researchers, and industry leaders dedicated to shaping the future of learning. LXD has emerged as a powerful bridge between learning design and educational technology, combining pedagogical expertise with technological innovation to create engaging, learnercentred experiences. This conference provides a unique platform for sharing cutting-edge practices, innovative approaches, and emerging trends in LXD across diverse educational and professional contexts. The conference programme includes keynote presentations, interactive workshops, panel discussions, session meetings and networking opportunities. We invite contributions from the following fields: 1) Learning Experience Design Foundations; 2) Learning Sciences and Pedagogy; 3) EdTech and Digital Innovation; 4) AI and the Future of Learning; 5) User Experience and Interface Design for Learning; 6) Workplace Learning and Professional Development; 7) Inclusion, Equity, and Accessibility. PARTICIPATION Conference language is English. To participate in the conference, please fill in a registration form on our website https://ld-initiatives.ch/en/conferences/ by March 05, 2027. The confirmation of conference participation will be sent within two weeks from the submission date but not later than March 12, 2027. Conference fee: 100 CHF. For participation in the conference both speakers and listeners will be awarded with ECTS credits (1 ECTS credit for speakers and 0,5 ECTS credits for listeners), which will be stated in the certificate of attendance. Certificates of attendance will be issued to all participants, including speakers and attendees, contingent upon their physical presence at the conference. REQUIREMENTS FOR SPEAKERS Agenda: plenary speeches - 30 minutes, section speeches - 15 minutes; discussion time - 10 minutes. PUBLICATION OF ABSTRACTS All conference participants may submit abstracts for publication. Abstracts will be published in the e-Book of Abstracts containing ISBN and DOI numbers. The language of publication is English. The deadline for abstracts submission is March 05, 2027. Abstracts must be downloaded in the registration form on our website: https://ld-initiatives.ch/en/conferences/. The abstract must contain 250-300 words, excluding references. The abstract must be preceded by the name(s) of the author(s), including their title(s), affiliation, and country (all in the abovementioned order). The title of the abstract must be in bold, all capitals, centered. References, if applicable (presented in the end of the abstract), and in-text citations must conform to the APA Style. All submitted abstracts will be subjected to the double-blind peer review process. The editorial board reserves the right to decline papers that contain plagiarized content or do not conform to the norms and subject matter of the conference. ENQUIRIES For any questions concerning the conference, please contact Dr. Nataliia Gach at info@ldinitiatives.ch or by phone: +41 78 340 32 13. For more information visit our website https://ld-initiatives.ch/en/conferences/.
